body:
---


 Meet The Food System


[Table]
Summary: | This unit will introduce students to the food system. Students will map out the food system, examining the seed-to-table process of making Takis. Students will taste the homemade version of a popular processed food or a simple plant-based food. | 


[Table]
Objectives: | Students will learn more about the food system and reflect on their own relationships to food while considering how this system can be made more just and sustainable.


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Student-focused instruction, self-management, social awareness. | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: (10 mins) | Opening ritual (10 mins): |  | Raise your hand if you’ve ever had Takis. | Today we will talk about all the steps that have to happen in order for you to eat a Taki. | These steps are part of something called THE FOOD SYSTEM. | Define a system: | A system is a set of things, people or animals that work together as one. | There are some systems you may already know. | An ecosystem - | consists of living/nonliving components that work together as a single unit to keep nature balanced. Think about how plants get energy from the sun and we get our energy from eating plants - that is a part of an ecosystem |  | A game system- A bunch of moving parts that work together to power a video game console. |  | Turn to your neighbor and discuss some other systems you may have heard of. | Have each group share what they discussed with their partners |  | Now that you know what a system is, what do you think the food system is? | The | food system provides food to people! There are many different parts to the food system, including people, plants and animals, natural resources, and businesses. | Without the food system, none of us would be able to eat. | The food system has a lot of steps that make it possible to get food into our bellies. |  | Today we will learn more about this food system and consider our role in this system. |  |  | Engaging Activity #1: | Sequential Order | (10 mins): |  | Each group will receive a set of cards. Each card represents part of the food system. In this case, they are the parts of a system to make Takis. | When I say “Go” you must work with your classmates in small groups to put the white cards in sequential order from first to last. | Show some examples of the white cards. | Once the white cards are in an order you can agree on, you can add the blue cards anywhere you think they go. | Show some examples of the blue cards. | Blue cards represent resources. If you think one of the white cards needs one or more resources, place the blue cards next to it to show that. | Model the placement of blue and white cards. | This challenge will also allow us to work on our teamwork and communication skills. |  | Give students time to complete this. | Students share out how they placed cards together. The point is not so much to get the one correct arrangement but for them to think about all the parts of the food system and how they function together. |  | What If | : | Now that we have successfully grouped the food system cards in the correct order, I want you to consider “what if” scenarios. | I want you to consider what the impacts would be if one or more parts of the system were removed. What would happen if the water card were removed? | Give students a few minutes to work on that scenario. You can add additional “what if” scenarios, removing more cards, if you would like. |  |  | Engaging Activity #2: | Greenhouse Tasks | (15 mins) : | When we work in the greenhouse, what parts of the food system are we providing |  | Today we’re going to be doing some gardening to help keep our part of the food system running. | Pick one or more garden or garden-related tasks for students to work on. | Chopping compost | Watering greenhouse | Pruning dead leaves/ compost | Creating class decoration for greenhouse wall such as a mural/ drawing of food cycle/ environment |  | OR | Make home made takis! | Or | faux ritos! |  |  | Closing: (10 mins) | Give students a tasting of a simple, whole, plant based food: apples slices, orange sections, popcorn. And/or, the home-made takis if you made them | This food took fewer steps to get to you than the Takis in your exercise. What steps do you think were missing? | Fewer steps mean fewer resources were used. What resources do you think we saved by eating this simple food instead of a processed food like Takis? | Today you were introduced to the food system and you also worked together in a system! | 

Materials: 
Food system cards: Make enough so that each group has one of each of the white cards and 12 of each of the blue cards
Taki task cards
If making home made takis or faux ritos:
tooth picks
Corn tortillas
Canola oil 
Chili powder
Onion powder
Salt 
Cayenne
Sheet trays
oven
Plastic baggies

Prep work and setup:
Print and cut out cards
Prepare a tasting or prep the making of Takis

Anticipated problems:
Not enough time
No greenhouse to do greenhouse tasks
Downtime while takis are baking

Back pocket activities:
Have students act out the food system in mime or skit version
Ask students to draw and write about what they ate for breakfast and how it went from seed to their plate. Have students share with the class.

Task cards or handouts used: 
Task cards 
Recipe: Fuego Takis or Fauxritos

3. Farm Workers & Pesticides




[Table]
Summary: | Students will read a story to learn about how pesticides negatively affect farm workers and learn about the activist work of Cesar Chavez. Students will then make a collective mural to show what they’ve learned about the connections between pollinators, farm workers, and themselves. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will understand that the agriculture industry does not always prioritize the health of farm workers, and that activism creates change.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, cultural diversity. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson positions the teacher as facilitator by supporting students in reading comprehension and facilitating students’ participation as activists.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | This lesson teaches social awareness by developing empathy and compassion for farm workers. It also teaches relationship skills by giving students practice in communication and activism.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson invites students to think about the ways that insects and plants interact in the garden, and differentiate between insects that help the garden and pests that hurt the garden. This will also help students make connections between growing food in their garden and farms that grow food on larger scales.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students will think critically about stewardship over land – how it is important to care for plants and insects and extend that care to human labor rights and understand that there is a way to take care of the land that nourishes rather than exploits our labor. Students will see an example of activism.


[Table]
Tags: |  | Spring Garden Jobs, Seed starting/making soil, immigration, celebrating cultural heritage, California, North America, Central America, farmland | Stewardship, crops, pesticides, insects, pollinators, weeding, watering, mulching, sifting compost | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow, Day 1: ( | 45 minutes | ) | Opening ritual (10 minutes): | “Can someone tell me what we discussed last class, and what pesticides are? Remind students that pesticides are harmful chemicals that are used to kill bugs, but can have effects on the rest of the garden food web.” | “Last time we mentioned briefly how pesticides don’t only hurt the land, bugs, and animals, but they can hurt farm workers too. Today we are going to talk a little bit more about that and learn about a person who fought for the rights of farm workers.” | Have students guess how pesticides might affect the human body. Explain that pesticide exposure over a long time can lead to illnesses in lungs and skin, or even cause cancer. | Define a farm worker as a person who works on a farm, but doesn’t own it or make decisions about how the farm is run, such as whether or not pesticides are used. | Show comprehension questions for students to pay attention to during the reading. Read aloud questions beforehand: | How did Cesar feel when he lived on the ranch with his family? | How did Cesar’s life change when Cesar and his family became migrant farm workers? | What are some of the challenges that Cesar faced as a farm worker? | How did Cesar demand change for himself and other farm workers?  Did he do it alone? | What do farm workers have in common with bees and other pollinators? How are they connected? |  | Engaging activity #1 (15 minutes): | Read sections of “Harvesting Hope” by Kathleen Krull. As you read, pause to have students answer the conversation questions, either in a whole group or as a pair share. | Highlight the following points as you read: | “The book uses the word ‘migrant workers’ to describe Cesar’s job. Migrant workers travel from place to place working on different farms. Many migrant farmers are also immigrants, which means they have come from one country to another. Maybe people become immigrants because their home isn’t safe, or because they are looking for better opportunities.” | Ask students if they remember how much Cesar was being paid for his work. “You may know from working in our garden that farming can be hard work! You have to bend over for long periods of time and work in all kinds of weather. Now imagine you are doing this all day long and only getting paid thirty cents. How would that make you feel?” | While reading, explain what a strike and a boycott are and how they are used as tools to demand change for workers. | Explain that while Cesar Chavez and other farm worker leaders have improved life for farm workers, they still face many challenges. They still are not very well paid, and they still can face risks from pesticide exposure on farms. They may not know if pesticides are being used. |  | Engaging activity #2 (15 minutes): | “We know that Cesar was really proud of the work that he did as a farm worker from the beginning of the story. He was proud to grow food for others, but he and other farm workers deserve to be safe and have choice about their working conditions. Today we are going to give you all a choice about what garden job you want to do, just like farm workers deserve choices in their working conditions.” | Give students two or more options for a garden job. Give instructions on how to do the jobs, and let students do the job that most appeals to them. Suitable jobs might be weeding, watering, mulching, or sifting compost. |  | Closing ritual (5 minutes): | “We will keep talking next time about the connections between bees, pollinators, farm workers, and us.” | Share a seasonal tasting. |  | Agenda/Class Flow, Day 2: (45 minutes) |  |  | Opening ritual (15 minutes): | Ask students to share back what we learned last time about farm workers and some of the challenges they face. | “What do farm workers have in common with bees and other pollinators? How are they connected?” Take student answers. | “Today, farm workers are still fighting for their rights, for better pay, and safer working conditions. Do you remember how in the book, Cesar and his supporters went on a march to teach others about their concerns and to demand change? Another way that leaders can educate others and demand change is through artwork.” | “Like Cesar Chavez, the vast majority of farm workers in our country today are migrants from Mexico and Central America. There is a strong tradition in Mexico and Central America of using murals for social change. Can anyone tell me what a mural is? A mural is a large piece of art, sometimes made by one artist or sometimes made by many people.” | Pass out examples of mural images. Have students share in pairs to answer the following questions: | What do you see in the mural? | What do you think the artist is trying to tell us in the mural? |  | Engaging activity #1(25 minutes): | Explain that we are going to make our own mural as a grade to teach others about what we’ve learned during garden class this spring and how farm workers and pollinators are connected. “Cesar Chavez raised awareness about the challenges that farm workers face, and that was what helped other people support their fight.” | Students have the choice of making either a flower, a bee or other pollinator, or farmworker to add to our mural.  The teachers will assemble all the pieces together to make our final mural. | “The Mexican and Central American muralists often include text to reinforce their message. On your piece of the mural, write a sentence that makes the connection between farm workers and pollinators, or something else that stood out to you during garden class this spring.” Write up sentence starters on the board: | Farm workers deserve… | Farm workers and bees are connected because… | Bees are important because … | Give students a chance to work on their piece of the mural. Circulate to help as needed.  Students can also add other pieces to the mural that they see as appropriate. |  | Closing ritual (5 minutes): | Have students share what they wrote and drew for their piece of the mural. | Thank students for helping to contribute to helping others learn about farm workers, pollinators, and the connections between them and ourselves. | End the lesson with a seasonal tasting. | 

Back pocket activity: 
Have students make labor related signs for the cafeteria based on what they learned about Cesar Chavez and the farmworkers movement (Farm workers deserve… We appreciate our farmworkers, Farmworkers and  bees help us…, etc.)
Write Thank You cards to cafeteria workers for helping us bring food from farm → schools
Make stickers with similar messaging to give out to students at school (“I am grateful for pollinators” “I am grateful for farmworkers”) 

Materials: 
Seasonal tastings, one for each day
Harvesting Hope: The Story of Cesar Chavez by Kathleen Krull (picture book from Amazon or YouTube read-aloud)
Materials for garden job in day one
Mural visuals 
Paper, markers, paint, or other art materials of your choosing

Prep work and setup:
Prepare tastings for each day.
Set up garden jobs for day 1.
Write comprehension questions for reading on board for day 1.
Set out and organize art materials for day 2. 

Anticipated problems:
Be prepared to correct student misconceptions during the reading.
The reading can take more or less time, depending on the depth of conversation. Make sure the garden job is flexible in terms of time needed to complete it. 

Background information for instructor:
The murals shown in the visuals come from WNYC, Inner Sunset Park Neighbors, and Juana Alicia.
Readings on injustices against farm workers are available here:
PAN
Exposed and Ignored
Pesticide laws need reform
A deeper dive for older students on Cesar Chavez’s life and the farm worker rights movement is available from PBS here.

3K-1 Indoor Garden Jobs


[Table]
Summary: | Students will engage in a | n indoor garden job like soil mixing, seed saving, or seed starting. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to understand that there are many ways to help the garden, both inside and outside.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson communicates high expectations by conveying faith in students to complete the task in the allotted time. The lesson also reshapes curriculum by promoting groupwork and movement.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students will practice relationship skills by being calm and cautious with plants and animals. There is typically less room in indoor spaces so students will have to stay calm while doing a focused task like measuring soil or carefully planting a seed.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson emphasizes the importance of garden tasks that are done indoors, and that can support the garden later on.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to complete an indoor garden task on their own with a little guidance.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : indoor garden tasks, spring garden jobs, fall garden jobs | If gardening | : | soil, seeds, compost, stewardship | For 216: | Indicate if lesson is co-taught and/or double period | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: ( | 45-50 minutes | ) | Opening ritual (10 mins) |  | Welcome students back to garden class. For K-1, go over expectations and petal points update: We are kind, safe, brave, and respectful.  Choose which petal point they are working towards this class.  This lesson especially emphasizes kindness when sharing and respect for others. | Remind students that all year, we’ve been learning what it means to be a gardener. Ask students some ways that they have learned to be a gardener. Solicit student responses. We’ve also learned WHY gardening is important and why we like to do it. Ask students about how gardening makes them feel (good, calm, happy, excited, etc) |  | Today we are inside because there are actually a lot of ways we can help the garden inside too! We are going to practice being calm for our garden job today as we work inside to help the garden get ready for spring and summer. |  |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): | Pick one job from below (or create your own!) and explain to students how the job is going to work: | For soil mixing: | Ask students what plants need to survive. Explain that you will be making a soil recipe to plant seeds in. The soil needs to be the perfect mix of each ingredient to make the seeds happy and healthy. | Help students make the connection between measuring ingredients in kitchen class and measuring soil ingredients today. | Emphasize that in order to do the right mix of soil, we will have to be calm with our bodies as we focus to measure the right ingredients and keep our workspace clean. | For seed starting: | Frame the lesson around the coming of spring and the need to start baby seeds inside where it is warmer so they can be big and strong when its time to put them in the ground. | First, students can decorate their cups or pots with stickers and crayons.  Make sure they put their names on it or ask their classroom teacher to help. | Demonstrate how to fill the cup, make a hole, and bury the seed. Have students help show how this is done. | Emphasize that we need to be calm as we put our seed in the soil. You can have students make wishes for their seeds as they tuck them away to grow! | For seed saving: | Ask students where plants come from. Seeds! Explain that an important process in gardening is to save the seeds that our plants make so that we can grow them again. Some seeds are small and some are big, so we need to be calm so that we can save as many as possible. | Hand out seed envelopes and colored pencils for students to decorate or to take home. |  | Closing ritual with SEL component (10 mins) | Have students wash hands and come back together. | Ask students how it felt to practice being calm. Ask again how doing garden activities makes students feel. | Distribute a seasonal tasting.  Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
For 3k/pk, seed starting is most successful when done one step at a time with lot of guidance.  Teacher can walk around with a small watering can to finish!
These are all good activities to combine with an Earth Day event.

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity: 
What a plant needs to survive worksheet

Materials: 
Based on the activity you’ve chosen, you will need:
For soil mixing: 
ingredients to soil (perlite, vermiculite, compost, coir), 
small cups for measuring
trays to measure into
hand washing station
For seed starting: 
Trays
Markers, crayons
Stickers (whatever you have on hand!)  
Seeds (radishes, peas work well)
pre mixed soil in paper bowls
Spoons for scooping soil
cups  to start the seeds in
popsicle sticks and pencils to write the seed type
spray bottles with water or small watering can
For seed saving: 
trays 
Assorted seeds
seed envelopes
colored pencils

Prep work and setup:
Prep activity of your choice.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:


Photos, if applicable:

A Visit to
Mashama Bailey’s
Restaurant

[Table]
Summary: | Students will first watch a video about Chef Mashama Bailey and how her heritage has shaped her cooking, and then students will cook and eat one of her recipes. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will make connections between their previous knowledge about African American foodways, the personal story of a present-day African American chef, and themselves.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, cultural diversity. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Highlights Black American culture


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Bravery, kindness, respect, collaboration, pride, joy, empathizes with others from diverse backgrounds and cultures


[Table]
Garden Connection: How does this lesson make use of the garden? | 


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | 


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: | food cultures, chefs, cooking, stove (heat), corn, grits, Southern cooking (American Southeast) | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Ask students to recall what they learned in the previous lesson, during the African American food museum exhibit. If this lesson does not follow the African American food museum lesson or if students need reminders, tell students that today you are learning about Black American culture and how much influence it has on American culture as a whole and American food in particular. | Explain that today we are going to watch a video about Chef Mashama Bailey, an African American chef in the state of Georgia.  Tell students that she was born in Georgia, moved to New York, then came back to Georgia and made her own restaurant. She is a chef who is alive today. | Show students this | slideshow | or use the slides as pictures to explain some helpful background to the video: | Explanation about of the James Beard award | Explanation that Southern food was invented by Black cooks | Explanation of segregation/Jim Crow and the fact that Mashama Bailey’s restaurant is located in a former bus station that was segregated. | Explanation of the dessert Mashama Bailey serves all diners at her restaurant: a thrill. | Watch | “Meet Mashama Bailey” | on “Jenna and Friends” until minute 3:52 or minute 5:31. Before you show the video, tell the students to listen for things they connect to or find interesting and to be ready to ask any questions. | Check for questions, go over any points you think should be repeated from the video, | Tell students that we are going to make a recipe from Mashama Bailey today. Mashama got this recipe from another famous African American chef named Edna Lewis, and she adapted her recipe for corn grits. We are also going to make collard greens, another important ingredient in African American cooking traditions. | Engaging activity: | Divide students into two groups: one small group to add seasoning to the grits and one large group to make the collard greens. Give students jobs and tools. Supervise and circulate among students to help them with the cooking jobs. | Closing ritual: | Share the tasting together. | 

Grade Level Variations:
3K and PK can cut collards with scissors but this should be ready in advance in case they don’t get very far. Pre-make grits
K-5: Teacher should be with the grits pot and supervise the stirring
6-8: Students may be able to be at the pot without supervision

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity:
Students can work together to think of a “special dish” that they envision could be added to the menu of Mashama Bailey’s restaurant, based on what they’ve learned about her food story and cooking.
Upper elementary and middle school students can do the special dish worksheet
Younger kids can do the design-your-own-popsicle coloring sheet. You can remind them that the dish that Mashama Bailey serves at her restaurant, a thrill, is a kind of homemade popsicle

Materials: 
Projector and computers to share video
Cups and spoons for tasting
Ingredients
Printed recipes
Written Task sheets for corn grits and stewed greens
Visual Task Cards for collard greens and grits
Trays
Garlic graters
Tablespoons
Large stirring spoons
Bowls
Pot
Induction burner
Scissors

Prep work and setup:
Decide ahead of time how many groups of students you will have preparing the recipe. Decide what quantity of the recipe you want to make.
Pre-cook the corn grits. Dice one onion ahead of time. 
Set up the greens station with an induction burner and pot. 
For the corn grits group, prepare a tray with the following:
1 large bowl with cooked, hot grits
1 container of salt
Pepper grinder
Cheese
1 large stirring spoon
Task sheet
Cups and spoons for serving portions
For the collard greens group, prepare a tray with the following:
10 pairs of scissors
1 container of salt
1 container of sugar
1 container of red pepper flakes
1 bottle of oil
1 bottle of red wine or apple cider vinegar
1 pre-cut onion
Pepper grinder
2-3 bunches of collard greens
Task sheet

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Anticipated issue: The grits group will have much less work to do because the grits will need to be pre-cooked. You can have this group use spoons to start to serve the grits into cups, making one portion for each student. You may also assign fewer students to the corn grits group and have the majority of students work on the stewed greens.
Background Info: This lesson is intended to follow the African American Food Museum lesson. It is not intended to be a stand-alone lesson, since it will require students to have a basic understanding of African American foodways. This lesson could also exist as part of a larger unit on African American history and culture.
https://cherrybombe.com/blogs/radio-cherry-bombe/mashama-bailey-2025 

Photos, if applicable:

---


African American Food Traditions



[Table]
Summary: | Students will participate in a “museum tour” to see artifacts that tell about African American contributions to American food culture. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will learn about ingredients, dishes, and cooking techniques that were innovated by African American farmers and cooks and have become an essential part of American cuisine.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Multicultural Education


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by focusing on African American history. This lesson also reframes the curriculum by highlighting the importance of African American food traditions in American food.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills |  | This lesson has students practice social awareness in learning more deeply about injustice against African American people during American history, while recognizing the central role that African Americans have had in shaping American food culture. | 


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Can bring attention to crops/plants growing in the garden that are mentioned in the lesson


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students will be asked to assess their prior knowledge and understanding of African Americans’ roles in shaping food and farming culture. Students will be asked to think about food stories as a whole and where these African American stories and voices are and aren’t present (and why).


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : African American history and culture, United States | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes) | Opening ritual (15 minutes): | Show students the map of the world. Explain that here in the United States, our food is influenced by the many different food traditions from all over the world. Foods like empanadas, dumplings, and pasta were invented in other places, then brought over by immigrants. | Today, we are going to be learning about foods that were invented in America by African American farmers and cooks. People who are African American have heritage from the continent of Africa. Show the continent of Africa on the world map. | We are going to go on an imaginary field trip to the National Museum of African American History and Culture in Washington, D.C. (If possible, show an image of the NMAAHC on the board or in hand-out form.) | Explain that around the room, you’ll see pictures of cooks, farmers, and leaders of African heritage. “Since we are in kitchen class, we are specifically going to be learning about how African American farmers and cooks have influenced American food culture. We’ll be visiting a ‘food exhibit’ later in class to learn more.” |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Pass out food museum worksheets. Explain that we will be reading the “exhibit introduction” on their worksheet out loud. Ask them to listen along and be prepared afterwards to say if there was any new or surprising information for them in the reading. Take time after reading to answer student questions, correct misconceptions, and share what students learned. | Show students an example of the artifacts that will be on each table. Explain that they should read the tag for each artifact, which will tell them about an ingredient, dish, or cooking technique that we can attribute to African American farmers and cooks. | Show them how they will then write the underlined word on each tag on their worksheet, matching the correct number. | After students have finished the activity, ask: what surprised you to learn that it was invented by African American farmers and cooks? What new information did you learn today? | If time allows, students can read the visuals from the Modern Black Food & Farming Heroes Hall of Fame document, either by circulating around the room or at their tables. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Ask students to share out something they learned from the museum of African-American history–something that surprised them, moved them, etc. | Tell students they were historians today, making sure that an important part of American history was told. | Share a tasting with the class | 

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity: This lesson could exist as an introduction to a larger unit on African American contributions to American food culture. Other topics could include: the role of food in the civil rights movement; the invention of free breakfast programs by civil rights groups like the Black Panthers; regional food specialities influenced by African American food innovations; and studies of modern-day African American farmers and cooks.

Materials: 
World map
Museum artifacts with labels 
Artifact exploration worksheets, one per student
Modern Black Food & Farming Heroes Hall of Fame visuals 
If students are taking recipes to go: plastic containers, plastic bags 

Prep work and setup:
@@Hang visuals from the Hall of Fame document around the room. Make additional copies to pass around for students to look at or read

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
These resources provide deeper information on African American include and were instrumental in the writing of this lesson:
The National Museum for African American History and Culture website
The book African American Foodways, edited by Anne L. Bower
The blog Afroculinaria, written by African American food historian Michael Twitty

Photos, if applicable:

  Agua Fresca


[Table]
Summary: | Students will learn a bit about Agua Fresca and make and taste different agua fresca flavors. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will know what Agua Fresca is and where it comes from, Students will be able to prepare their fruits safely, Students will use the smoothie bike, if there is one, to blend up the drink, Students will work together to clean up the classroom.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | List all that apply: | kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Aguas Frescas are a very popular drink in Mexico and Central America - many students will be able to connect culturally and personally.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | This lesson promotes self management if students are showing self confidence in sharing a personal anecdote about aguas frescas, and by expecting students to control their impulses by waiting to eat until everyone is served. This lesson promotes relationship skills by expecting that students will willingly share tools, work well together in groups, and make connections to outside life. This lesson should promote responsible decision making by expecting that students will clean up after themselves, use tools safely, and consider the safety and well being of others (especially while using the blender bike)


[Table]
Garden Connection: How does this lesson make use of the garden? | This lesson can happen in the garden if blenders and materials are brought outside. A great lesson to celebrate the end of the year. Mint can also be harvested from the garden and used in this recipe.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson supports critical thinking by having students write out their own recipe, either remembering the ingredients or creating their own based on what they've already made.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : Mexico, Carribean, Central America | If cooking | : chopping, blending, fruit, peeling, juicing | 



[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand | -outs and | include estimated time) | Opening ritual with SEL component: | Welcome students to class | Introduce Agua Fresca - ask students if they’ve tried it before - if so what flavors have they tried, if they know where it’s a very popular drink (Mexico, Caribbean, Central America), etc. What do they think the difference will be between aguas frescas and a fruity water such as infused water (which my students made a few weeks earlier during a sugar lesson - could skip if you’re going to have to explain what an infused water is) or a smoothie. Tell students that aguas frescas are meant to be refreshing and are a great, delicious, way to get hydrated and to get lots of vitamins. |  | Engaging activity: | Have students prep ingredients for the aguas frescas. Cucumbers need to be peeled and cut into chunks. Mint needs to be rinsed and de-stemmed. Strawberries need to be rinsed and cut into halves or thirds, and limes need to be juiced. |  | Cucumber Recipe: 4-5 cucumbers, 6 limes, 2 tsp sweetener (or to taste), lots of mint! |  | Strawberry Recipe: 2 pounds strawberries, 4 limes, 2 tsp sweetener (or to taste), mint |  | Add all the ingredients to a blender, add about a half cup of water and blend until smooth. Add to a pitcher with a little more water (depending how big your pitchers are and how watery your aguas frescas already seem to be) add ice and serve. |  | Closing ritual with SEL component: | Once students have had a chance to taste and compare the two different flavors, and the tables have been cleared off, pass out the blank recipe cards and have students try to come up with their own recipes based on what they just tasted: For example, they may alter one of the recipes you just made by adding or subtracting quantities of an ingredient or adding a new ingredient all together, or they may come up with a whole new flavor on their own. Depending on the age you’ll have to scaffold to varying degrees to get students to be able to conceptualize how much fruit vs. liquid vs. herb vs. sweetener makes sense for a recipe. Before students leave ask for a couple volunteers to share their recipes.


Grade level variations:
For grades 1-2:
Have students color the coloring sheets after making the recipe, if time allows.  
Students follow verbal instructions as a class. Instead of every student doing every job, each table will have its own job. 
Ex: one table chops cucumber, one cuts mint with scissors, one juices limes, etc.
For grades 3-5:
Have students make their own recipes on the blank recipe cards, either creating a new version of the recipe or working together to remember the ingredients and steps.
Students follow instructions independently using task cards

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
ADW: Armadillidium vulgare: INFORMATION
8 Facts About the Fascinating Roly-Poly
Some important roly poly facts (can use in slideshow presentation!):
Aka pillbugs, potato bugs, doodle big, basketball bug, little pig
Crustaceans, molt, have gills (can’t breathe underwater or in very dry conditions), 7 pairs of legs, tiny hairs all over their bodies to sense
Nocturnal, prefer wet conditions, mostly eat dead plants but sometimes eat healthy plants if they are very wet. They love strawberries!
Females have a pouch that holds hundreds of eggs and carries her hatched babies for a few weeks
Predator defenses: hard shell, curls into a ball, produce chemicals like a skunk to repel predators (birds, ants, spiders, amphibians, etc), camouflage 
They can live in unhealthy soil with toxic heavy metals like lead and break it down in their gut. Scientists observe them to see how much metal is in the environment/how healthy the ecosystem is.

Explain that squirrels balance on thin branches and hop from tree to tree when they are looking for food.  Today, we are going to explore the garden by hopping in between the hula hoop circles, just like a squirrel in a tree.  Show students how to stay in the hoops, and what to do if you “fall” off the trees (hop right back in!).  While we are hopping, we are looking for seeds in the garden.  Each squirrel should pick one seed from each bowl.  Hand out baskets or cloth bags for students to collect seeds in. | Walk around the garden, guiding students to stay in the “trees” while they are exploring today.  Help them identify the different types of seeds in their baskets. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Have students bring their seeds back to a collective bucket or bowl.  Show students how many seeds they collected today.  Pat yourself on the back if you were kind to others when looking for seeds today. | Ask students:  What does a squirrel eat in the garden? | Finish with a tasting, ideally of something that squirrels also eat, like seeds or dried fruit.  Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
n/a
Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: If some students finish early, have them empty their bowl in the collective bucket and start again.  If all students finish early, collect seeds and set up Squirrel's garden coloring page on clipboards.  Alternatively, have some seed bins, popsicle sticks and magnifying glasses prepped so students can look at seeds up close.  

Materials: 
Squirrel finger puppet 
Squirrels (Nonfiction) by Emily Rose Townshend OR 
12pk 24" hula hoops, or borrow from gym class, or sidewalk chalk if you have concrete floors in garden space
Acorns, large seeds (corn, pumpkin seeds in shell, sunflower seeds, dried beans), dried fruit, cereal?
8-10 large plastic trays  to put seeds on
Baskets, small bags , or cups for students to collect seeds in (use whatever you have on hand)
Seed or dried fruit tasting

Prep work and setup: Draw out chalk circles, or lay out hula hoops in the garden space.  It’s ok to have some hoops touching edges, and some about a foot apart so they can hop between “trees”.  Set up trays with different kinds of seeds that squirrels eat (sunflower seeds, acorns, corn, pumpkin seeds, dried beans).  You could also add dried fruits or vegetables, as long as you remind students beforehand that they are collecting food for squirrels, not eating it.  

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Some squirrel facts: 


Make sure you have enough hoops in the garden so there aren’t too many “traffic jams” (It’s good to space them out in a loop or triangle pattern).  Explain to students that we say “excuse me” if we need more space, and wait our turn before jumping into a crowded hoop.  
Hoops are for hopping into in this class, not picking up!  Students may need an extra reminder about this :) 
If class must happen indoors, intro with the same squirrel behaviors activity, and then hand out trays and seeds for students to look at.  Print page 4 of this activity (can you find the seeds) and place in sheet protectors.  Students can match the different seeds to the correct image on their sheet. Finish with a coloring page.

 Applesauce 



[Table]
Summary: | Students make applesauce and taste different varieties of apples.


[Table]
Objectives: | Students will be able to explain the ingredients used to make applesauce. | 


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Kitchen skills and related academic content, cultural diversity. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Promotes student-centered instruction and communicates high expectations as students complete cooking tasks within a certain time frame.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | relationship skills and self-management in taking risks and trying new foods.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Explain to students that apples grow in trees, usually in apple orchards, and that there are many apple orchards in upstate New York where there is more space. Apples are ready to harvest in the fall, many people go apple picking and make lots of yummy foods with apples, including applesauce.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to practice their knife skills by chopping apples and allows for independence by exploring what flavors and tastes they like.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | Fall harvest recipe, origin is Central Europe/England, also popular in America from European immigrants especially in Amish culture |  | Cooking Skills: | Cutting, measuring, mixing, stirring | Main ingredients: Apples, lemon juice, water, cinnamon, brown sugar or honey | Heating element: Stove | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (5 minutes): | Introduce the applesauce recipe. | Go over ingredients and make sure there are no allergies. | Ask students to raise their hands if they like apples. Ask them how does an apple grow? In a tree? On the ground? In the ground? | Show | pictures of apple trees | and explain that apples grow on trees in apple trees, and that there are many apple orchards in upstate New York. Ask students if they have ever been apple picking? What is their favorite type of apple? How do they like eating apples? Plain? In a pie? Applesauce? | Explain that the origin of applesauce is from Central Europe/England but people make applesauce all over America too, especially in Amish communities. Leave time for students to ask questions about apples and applesauce and share any relevant family stories or recipes, especially if they have been apple picking or grown apples. |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): | Students cut up pieces of apple into small pieces. | Add cut apple pieces to a pot with a little bit of water and cook over medium/low heat. Add cinnamon and a bit of honey or brown sugar (It didn’t make sense to me to measure any of this - add less cinnamon for K since they tend to not like flavor, older kids will probably want more cinnamon. The amount of sweetener depends on how sweet your apples are - start with a little and taste as you go. You only need a small amount of water (think: a couple tablespoons?) so that the apples don’t burn - keep an eye on it and mix every once in a while. | With older students, give them picture references of how big the apples should be cut. You also can allow them to measure out the spices and give them an introduction about measuring spoons and being careful with spices. | While applesauce cooks, pass out slices of different apple varieties for students to taste. | Hand out one variety at a time. Tell students to wait before tasting. In the meantime, they can touch and smell the apple slice. Count down 3 ,2,1 and everyone takes a bite together so you can hear the class crunch. Repeat with other apple varieties and have students vote on a favorite and compare how each variety tastes. | Pass out coloring sheets for students to work on as applesauce cooks. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, bon appetit, let’s eat! Students vote with thumbs to show how they liked it. Ask students if there’s anything else they would add to the recipe and if there are any other foods that would be paired well with.

Grade Level Variations:
3K-PK - pre-cut some or all of the apples depending on their comfort using knives. Practice cutting with play dough if necessary. Pre measure spices. Prepare applesauce before class just in case. Spend more time discussing how apples grow, acting out growing from a seed into a tree, picking an apple from a tree. Discuss why eating fruits is important.

K-1 - Pre-cut some of the apples or do a demonstration to show the sizing of the apples. Emphasize the different spices and importance of adding in spices slowly to make sure you don’t use too much. Focus on tasting the different applesauces with different types of spices. Encourage students to try new spices and foods. Introduce measuring spoons and measuring carefully, have them practice a few times before doing it in the real recipe.

2-3 - Have kids do most of the cutting, measuring and mixing by themselves. Have one or two students come up to help mix the applesauce while the rest of them are coloring or doing the word search. Focus on stove safety. Get more into the cultural origins of applesauce and encourage students to share family stories about foods they eat at home with apples and talk about cross cultural use of fruits, especially apples.

Older students - Focus more on the cultural significance and origins of apples and applesauce, as well as the food production and distribution systems - how do apples grow and how do they get to us? Why can’t we grow a lot of them in the city? What do apples and appletrees need to grow? What are the positive and negative effects of getting them from closer vs. further away?

Materials: 
Apple sauce ingredients (apples, lemon juice, cinnamon, water, honey, brown sugar)
Knives
Cutting Boards
Peelers (optional)
Bowls
Apple cutters
Hot plate
Pot
Coloring sheet / wordsearch
Colored pencils / crayons / pencils
Blender (or mashers)
Take home recipes

Prep work and setup:
Wash apples, peel apples (optional: only if you want that class to eat the applesauce that they make, otherwise have backup already made for tasting)
Print out word searches or coloring pages 
Print and organize take home recipes

Anticipated problems:
Applesauce won’t have time to cook/will be too hot to eat (have each class make applesauce for the next class)

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: 
Read the apple story and do apple story coloring sheets.
After discussing the path from seed to table of another food (peanut butter, cheese, a carrot), students can write and illustrate this food’s story.

Task cards or handouts used: 
A is for apple coloring page
Apple Word Search Printable | Worksheets & Printables

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand | -outs and | include estimated time) | Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson | . (5 min) | Welcome back to kitchen class; besides thanking gardeners and cooks, who else helps us enjoy the food we eat? (Truckers, delivery drivers, farmers, butchers, bakers, factory workers, grocery store workers, dishwashers, BEES) | Today, we will make blueberry muffins and learn about how bees help to feed us. |  | Engaging activity: | Make blueberry muffins (15 min) | Keep an eye on cook time; lower the oven temp after 5 min (see recipe) | While the muffins are baking, discuss bees and bee pollination. (10 min) | NATIVE BEES | 4,000 native bee species in the United States (there were no honey bees in America before European settlers brought hives from Europe). | Native bees pollinate 80 percent of flowering plants and approximately | 75 percent of the fruits, nuts, and vegetables | grown in this country. | The southeastern blueberry bee, Habropoda laboriosa, capable of visiting as many as 50,000 blueberry flowers and pollinating enough of them to produce more than 6,000 ripe blueberries. At the market those 6,000 blueberries are worth approximately $20 or more. | Some species are solitary (living and raising their brood alone) and some are social (living together and sharing the rearing and provisioning). | The anthers of the blueberry flower are tubular with an opening pore only at one end. The southeastern blueberry bee attaches herself to the blueberry flower and vibrates her flight muscles very rapidly. Pollen comes out of the opening of the anther and is collected by the southeastern blueberry bee. When she moves on to the next blueberry flower, her buzz pollination not only shakes out the pollen but causes pollen clinging to her body to attach to the stigma and pollinates the flower. | HONEY BEES |  | 10-80k bees per hive, depending on season | 1 queen bee, worker bees are females and have stingers | Male bees have no stingers, are only for reproduction, and are kicked out of the hive during winter months | Produce honey by mixing nectar with bee “spit” (full of enzymes and antibacterial) | COLONY COLLAPSE |  | Cause is unknown, but may be due to a combination of factors including diseases, nutrition, habitat loss, stress, and pesticides. | Many of the same factors affecting honey bee health are also affecting native bee species health as well. | Honey bees may be contributing to the extinction of some native bees. Honey bees can out-compete many native bees and extract vast amounts of pollen and nectar from every habitat in which they live. | Habitat loss and fragmentation are adversely affecting populations of native bees. Pesticides may also have an impact on many species including native bees. This became dramatically apparent in New Brunswick, Canada, years ago when the blueberry crop was nearly wiped out despite the fact that the plants appeared healthy. | It was determined that the program to control the spruce worm in nearby forests had almost eliminated the native bees. Blueberry farmers started litigation that went all the way to the Supreme Court of Canada. Eventually, they succeeded in having the Government place restrictions on the use of pesticides. It took a number of years before the local populations of native bees were restored, and blueberry crops became plentiful once again | School gardens like ours and community gardens are a great way to protect bees in NYC where they don’t have as many flowers to eat from. We plant lots of flowers specifically for the bees and don’t use any pesticides or fertilizers that would hurt them. | 3. (If time allows) Beeswax lip balm or pollinator garden planting (10-15 min) | Make beeswax lip balm | Support native bees by planting native plants |  | Closing ritual: | (5-10 min) | Tasting - Thank you, gardeners; thank you, cooks; thank you BEES! | We talked about how we can appreciate the bees who help make fruits for us - how can we appreciate other people who help feed us, including our parents and friends?

Berry Rosehip Bars
(Valerie Segrest, task card and coloring sheet images may be restricted)


[Table]
Summary: | Students bake bars with berries and rosehips and learn about foods native to North America. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to measure dry and wet ingredients and identify fruits that come from North America.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by deepening students’ knowledge about Native American foodways.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | Students practice relationship skills by taking turns to mix batter and to measure ingredients.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | The original recipe by Chef Sean Sherman utilizes mulberry. The red mulberry is native to New York State and even grows in or near some of our edible schoolyard gardens. Other berries grown in our gardens could also be used for this recipe (blackberries, raspberries, juneberries).


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students deepen their sense of place by considering the fruits native to New York state. Students consider the importance of indigenous environmental stewardship. Students will independently work to complete the recipe.


[Table]
Tags: | Native American, baking, mixing, measuring, fruit, berries, native foods, oven, stove, vegan | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (5 minutes): |  | Review community expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” | “We have been learning this month about Native American people, their cultures, and their food traditions.” Remind students that Native American people were the first people on the American continent, arriving thousands of years before European colonists. Have students share what they remember from previous lessons. | “Native American people grew food on their farms, and they also found foods that grew wild in nature to eat. Some of the foods that were growing wild in North America are foods we still eat today. Because of Native American knowledge, we have access and can enjoy these same foods today.” |  | Engaging activity (35 minutes): | Have students work in small groups to make the batter for the bars. Student jobs can include: | Measuring oats, canola oil, raisins, and sunflower butter | Greasing the baking tray | Mixing the batter | Pouring the batter into the baking tray | While the bars are baking, choose one of the grade level variation activities, described below, depending on your students’ age. Take out bars and give them about five minutes to cool before cutting. You can invert the pan to tip out the bars, then cut them into pieces to serve. | See recipe | here | . |  | Closing ritual (5 minutes): | Eat the bars together. | Ask students to share in small groups or partners about any recipes they eat at home that contain any of the fruits we used today. Emphasize that these fruits are available to us because of the environmental stewardship of Native American people over time. | 

Grade level variations:
Grades 4-5: While the bars are baking, read the quotes from Valerie Segrest, a Native American writer and food expert from the Muckleshoot Tribe. Have each table, pair, or small group pull a discussion question from a grab bag. If students finish, they can choose another question. 
Grades 2-3: Explain that several ingredients that we used today are originally from North America. Native Americans across the continent used these wild foods. Have students color in the coloring sheet.
Grade 3: Additionally share with students that different foods grow in different regions of North America. Blueberries grow in the northeast, persimmons in the southeast, paw paws in the midwest, prickly pear in the southwest, and so much more! Why would one fruit grow in one place and not another? 
Grades K-1: Offer two fresh berries to compare, such as blackberries or raspberries. Have students taste and share tasting words. Students can also vote on their favorite. Use a whiteboard or chart paper to track votes for favorite berry. Students can practice adding the sums together or practice using math symbols like <, >, and = with the results. 
Grades 3K - PK: Offer two fresh berries for comparison. Students can identify the colors and share some other fruits they know/might eat at home. After tasting, students can draw their favorite fruit. 

Back pocket activity: 
Watch either the video about harvesting nettles or harvesting camas from the Seattle Times’ project on preserving indigenous foodways in the Pacific Northwest. Discuss with students:
What surprised you about what you saw in the video? What’s something new that you learned?
What did the people in the video say about why these foods are important to them? 
What questions do you have?
Are there foods in your family or your culture that are very special, like how these foods are special for Native American people?

Materials: 
8x8 baking pan
Bowls for mixing
Measuring cups and spoons 
Spatula for spreading
Plates or napkins for serving
Knife
Small pot for cooking berries
Parchment paper (optional)
Ingredients for recipe:
Frozen fruit, such as blueberries, blackberries, or raspberries 
Oats
Rosehips
Canola oil
Raisins
Sunflower butter

Prep work and setup:
Preheat the oven to 350 degrees.
Pre-soak rosehips in hot water. 
Begin cooking berries over the stove so they can be cool enough for students to mix.
Assemble trays for small groups. 
Prepare materials for secondary activity.
For 4th-5th grades, cut out discussion questions. Put in brown paper bags, cups, or bowls for your grab bag. Make copies of the reading.
For 2nd-3rd grades, make photocopies of the coloring sheet. 
For 3K-1st, wash berries for tasting
Print out take-home recipe
Print task cards 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
This recipe was developed through a partnership with the Indigenous Food Lab and the USDA. It combines native ingredients that can be foraged, along with pantry items available through the USDA’s Food Distribution Program on Indian Reservations (FDPIR). 
Chef Sean Sherman (Oglala Lakota) demonstrates the recipe here. The recipe is available here. Replace peanut butter with sunflower butter, and replace butter with canola oil when cooking with students. 
Food writer and nutritionist Valerie Segrest (Muckleshoot) writes here about the importance of native berries and rosehips. The reading for 4th-5th grade has excerpted quotes from these essays.

Black Panther Party and the Free Breakfast Program



[Table]
Summary: | Students will learn about the Black Panther Party’s free breakfast initiative as a means of resistance and change. | 


[Table]
Objectives: | Students will learn about | the Black Panther Party and make a breakfast dish.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, multicultural education. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by providing an example of leadership and resistance in the Black community. The Black Panther Party was an active part of NYC and fundamentally shaped the Black resistance movements in NYC.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | This lesson helps students to develop social awareness by learning about a social justice issue, food insecurity, and how leaders in the Black community organized to make change. Students will also practice responsible decision-making as they use tools safely and clean up after themselves.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson encourages students to think about ways that we can use fruits that we grow in the garden as recipes, to cook for ourselves and our community members. This creates a connection between the community garden as a space to grow food but also distribute this food as a form of mutual aid and activism.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to think critically about the Civil Rights movement as a movement from the past but a struggle that continues in the present. This helps students make connections between past activists and activist movements to their present day lives and communities and learn lessons from movements in the past that can be applied today. Students are invited to work independently and in groups to create this recipe in class and also think about ways that they can use food to help out other students or community members in need beyond class time.


[Table]
Tags: |  | Black History Month, Winter Recipe, Juneteenth, North America, Civil Rights, Racism, Black Panther Party, Free Breakfast Program, Mutual Aid, Accessibility, Food Justice | Cooking skills: Chopping, measuring, mixing, baking, serving, Main ingredients: Oats, Sugar, Oil, Salt, Cinnamon, Pumpkin seeds, Dried fruit, Coconut, Sliced fruit, yogurt, Heating element: oven.


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: ( | 45 minutes) |  | Opening ritual (20 minutes): |  | Welcome students to class. Wash hands and take seats. How is everyone feeling today? Show me with your thumbs. | “We’ve been talking in garden and kitchen class about ways that people have used food and gardens to make the world a better place. Today we are talking about one of the ways that food was important in the Civil Right Movement.” | Solicit student knowledge about the Civil Rights Movement. | What does equality mean? What are people fighting for when they fight for equality? (housing, food, health care, etc.) | “In the 1960s, Black students weren’t allowed to go to the same schools as white students. A group during the Civil Rights Movement called the Black Panthers wanted to help Black students in schools. They got donations and started serving breakfast to students for free. Because of their great idea, eventually the federal government realized it should give free breakfast for all students.” | What did you all eat for breakfast this morning? Who made your breakfast? Was it your mom, your older brother, the deli worker or the cafeteria staff? | Show | presentation | with photos and video, cuing them beforehand with questions about what to look for in the slideshow and/or video (ie, who were the Black Panthers? What were they fighting for? Why is free breakfast important?) Ask students beforehand to listen for surprising information or something new they learned. | “These people were part of a group called the Black Panthers.  They were a group led by Black men and women right here in New York (or to be more specific, The Bronx, Brooklyn). They saw that in their neighborhoods, many people didn't have enough money to buy breakfast. They wanted to change that, so they got together to make free breakfasts every day to give to their neighbors.  Because of their great idea, now all New York kids can get free breakfast at school!" | What do you see in these photos? What did you learn in the video? | Has anyone heard of the Black Panthers before? Do you want to share what you already knew or something new you learned? | If time, read age appropriate book (linked below). |  | Engaging activity (15 minutes): | “To celebrate the Black Panther Party’s achievement of bringing breakfast to all schools, we are going to be making a breakfast dish!” | Ask students if they have ever eaten granola before. What toppings do you put in your granola? Fruits? | “Granola is made with oats. It’s super fun to make because you can add whatever ingredients and spices you want.” | Explain that because the granola needs to cook in the oven, they will make the batch for the next class. | Pass out | task sheets | and have students read through their jobs. | Pass out trays with materials. | Once students make the granola mix, collect it. | Time permitting: give students cutting boards, knives, and bananas to cut into small pieces. | All bananas are added to a small bowl in the middle of the table. | Cooked granola is passed out. Students begin to make their own bowls with yogurt, granola, and cut fruit. |  |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Once bowls are assembled, eat together and say “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.” | Ask students how they feel when they forget to eat breakfast and why they think it is so important. | What is one new thing you learned about the Black Panthers today? What is one thing you could share with a younger student or family member? | You can also have students serve the granola to each other or to other students in the school, to simulate the Free Breakfast Program. Have students wear aprons and gloves and go over food safety before they do this. | 

Grade Level Variations:

3K-PK: Focus on the concept that everyone needs breakfast to have energy and be able to focus throughout the day. Read ABCs of the Black Panther Party. Pre prepare all the foods and have students add and mix them together and sing the mixing song. Prepare coloring sheets / apron decorating or another art project to do if there is time after preparing and making the food.

K-1: Focus on the concepts of the Civil Rights movement and racism. Why was it unfair that Black students could not go to the same schools as white students? Why did people fight for all students to be able to go to school together? Acknowledge the Panthers as activists that helped fight for equality and create schools that all students can attend today. Read ABCs of the Black Panther Party. Pre measure most ingredients but you can have students chop up dried or fresh fruit if you are using it. Encourage students to make posters about equality if you have extra time.

2-3: Focus on the Free Breakfast program as a form of community activism and mutual aid. Show students pictures/selections from the BPP comic book. Ask students if they have ever gotten free food from the cafeteria or ESY or another place in their neighborhood and ask them how it feels to be able to take home free food to their family. Focus on why healthy food should be free and accessible for all people. Introduce the idea that there is still racial inequality and food inequity today and ask them how this shows up in their school and neighborhood community. Focus on measuring and mixing and split them up into pairs or groups to do the task card jobs for the recipe.

4-5: Ask students to make connections between the Black Panther Party and other individuals or organizations that have fought for Civil Rights that they have learned about. What are some racial inequalities that still exist today? What are some ways we can contribute to changing these inequalities? How can we make food more accessible for all people? How can we use our school garden to grow food and feed people in our neighborhood? Definitely focus on the photos, video and any other background information like the BPP comic book. Ask students if they have any prior knowledge of the Panthers or if their parents or families have talked to them about this before. Have students prepare granola and serve it to younger students, the way that the Panthers would have run the Free Breakfast program. Focus on food as a way to create community, take care of people and bring people together.

Materials: 
Task sheet
Take home recipe 
Projector or Smart Board to watch video
Ingredients: oats, sugar, oil, salt, cinnamon, pumpkin seeds, dried fruit, coconut
Sliced fruit and yogurt for serving
Measuring cups and spoons
Knives and cutting boards
Bowls and mixing spoons
Spoons and cups or bowls for eating

Prep work and setup:
Print task sheets and put in protective sleeves.
Print and organize recipes.
Print readings.
Set up one tray per table with oats, pumpkin seeds, sugar, salt, cinnamon, oil, and measuring spoons and cups.
Have bowls and mixing spoons ready for each table.
Prepare a batch of granola ahead of time for each class. 
Cut bananas or other fruit into small pieces ahead of class.

If you make small groups of 5-6 students, we recommend that each small group make one batch of granola in order to feed a whole class.

You could have students decorate aprons/hats, especially if they will be serving breakfast to other students in the school. Encourage them to use symbols and messages from the Black Panther Party.

CSA stands for Community Supported Agriculture (write this on board).  A CSA is a way for people to gather together and help a farm in their community. (7 minutes). |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Today, we are going to pretend the school garden is like a farm, and we will prepare vegetables for everyone in our classroom community. Grade Level Variations:
To adjust for 3k-k, self-contained classes, or shorter periods, have students all focus on 1-2 jobs (could be teabag filling, tying flowers or herb bunches, or coloring the farmstand sign).  Students can also just color or decorate their CSA bags.  Have veggies or herbs ready ahead of time, and then add a veggie and whatever they worked on in class to the bags at the end.  

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 216
If there is not enough food for students to take home and to harvest for a weekly farmstand, you can have students just harvest for farmstand and decorate bags to give away in the farmstand. Or they can prepare pre-made bags for that week’s farmstand.

Back pocket activity: If some groups finish their task early, have them sit at the tables and decorate a CSA bag to take home.  Make sure they put their names on them!

Tea:  set out some dried herbs in a bowl, and enough teabags per student.    
Farmstand:  set out brown bags on a table with some sheets of shipping stickers.  Set tablecloths and cut paper for signs.

Remind them ways to be respectful and responsible for our classroom and garden spaces. |  |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Explain the different materials students will camouflage one bug to somewhere in the room, to a picture, or to somewhere made up! Explain that they will do this by painting their bug to try to match the colors or patterns that they see in that part of the room, garden or picture (if you choose a brown table with stripes, use brown paint to camouflage your bug to that table, etc.) | Explain that the bugs will dry here and once they are dry the students will get their bugs back and they can either take them home or find a special place in the garden where they can live and other students can learn about camouflage from them. | Variation: you can keep all bugs to use as classroom or garden decoration. | If students are keeping bugs: write names on a piece of paper and lay the bugs to dry atop each student's name so you know whose is whose. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Have students clean up their materials. Make sure to put brushes in cups of water when students are done using them so they don’t dry out and wash out brushes with soap and water thoroughly. Make sure to put down a table covering so paint doesn’t get on the tables. | End class with a seasonal tasting. Ask students to share something creative they saw a classmate doing. What designs did you see other students creating that you liked? Encourage students to be confident in their own work and supportive of their classmates’ work.

Grade Level Variations:
For older kids you can have them match their bug and paint to a picture and make the bug blend into the picture. Or they can paint the bug to hide somewhere in the room.
For younger kids, they can just enjoy painting their own bugs whatever color.
You can have the bugs dry and redistribute them to students to take home, or you can make them into class decoration and put them on a string.
You can prepaint bugs to hide around the classroom and camouflage them before students paint their own.

Back pocket activity: 
Hide plastic bugs around the classroom or around the garden in beds and give students a few minutes to search for the bugs, collect them and bring them back to the center of the classroom. You can also have half the students hide them while the other half closes their eyes and searches and then have them switch.
You can also play a game where the students stand in a circle shoulder to shoulder with their hands behind their backs and have them pass around one or two insects. Have one student stand in the middle and try to guess who is holding the insect (give them 3 guesses). Do a demonstration with you in the middle first so they understand the game.
You can give students magnifying glasses to look at the plastic insects up close and make observations and then have them walk around the garden and use the magnifying glasses to observe real insects.
Read a book about bugs and insects.
Hold worms from the worm bin and discuss how the worms camouflage themselves with the compost. Discuss who are predators of worms? Who do worms need to hide themselves from?

Materials: 
Paint brushes
Plastic bugs
Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
Option 5
Option 6
Paint (tempera is non permanent, acrylic is permanent. Use acrylic paint if the students will be leaving the bugs outside).
Paper plates to mix paint
 Plastic cups to put brushes in water after they are done using
Printed photos of example camouflage
Printed photos to match when painting
“Drying rack” - paper with student names
Towels to wipe down tables
Table covering (paper or plastic)
Optional: Aprons/smocks so students don’t get paint on themselves. If you are inside and it is safe you can let students take off shoes while they are painting.

Prep work and setup:
Buy bugs and paints/paintbrushes
Print any photos needed
Laminate pictures
Write "camouflage" and the definition on a whiteboard or piece of paper.

Background info for educators:
This is a good lesson to do before or after this one to expand on how bugs/animals protect themselves in nature.
This is also a good lesson for more context or backup activities.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand | -outs and | include estimated time) | Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson | . | 5 mins: | Welcome students to class - how is everyone feeling? | Today we’re going to be learning all about butterflies and how they grow - and you’ll all get to make a butterfly to keep! Butterflies are very helpful insects. They help all the plants in our garden to grow. Raise your hand if you think being helpful is a good thing … who wants to share an example of something you did recently that was helpful to someone else, maybe someone in your class, or your family, or your teacher? |  | Engaging activity: | Read aloud: 10 Minutes | Read your choice of butterfly book, make sure it discusses butterfly life cycle. (10-20 minutes) | As we saw in the book, butterflies come in every different color combination you can imagine! What color butterflies have you seen before? | Show students your example of a completed butterfly. What part is the clothespin? What about the colorful pipe cleaner? | Hand out materials and coffee filters - you and whatever classroom teachers/paras are there should go around helping students choose pipe cleaner colors and put butterflies together when students are done decorating their coffee filters. | When students have completed butterflies, explain to them that when butterflies visit flowers and travel around the garden, it helps all the plants to grow (which is great!!), let them fly their butterflies around the room visiting the different flower visuals that you’ve displayed. If the weather is nice, bring students outside to let their butterflies fly around the garden and visit flowers. |  | Closing ritual with SEL component: | Clean up (5 minutes) | Provide clear instructions on how students can help to clean up the classroom, reminder that being helpful is so wonderful and you are so appreciative of them, they’re being helpful just like butterflies are helpful in the garden! | Dismiss them in your own way - | who learned something new about butterflies today? Who was helpful today? Who’s proud of themselves for making such beautiful butterflies? Who’s excited to show a loved one their butterfly and tell them what they learned today?

Back pocket activity: 
Have a different book about butterflies on hand to read to end class if there is enough time for it. 
Speedy students can make a second butterfly 
If you are doing this lesson in the garden in the spring students can look around the garden for butterflies or plant flowers for future butterflies to come and visit
Butterfly coloring sheet

Materials: 
Coffee filters 
Clothespins
Colorful pipe cleaners
Markers or Crayons or Watercolor paints and brushes (whichever you’re most comfortable with or happen to already have)
A book about Butterflies: The very hungry caterpillar is a great one, or there's a NatGeo KiDS book “Butterflies” that talks about the life cycle but also how butterflies survive (camouflage), what they eat, how many different beautiful color combinations they come in, etc.
A butterfly life cycle visual

Prep work and setup:
Cut pipe cleaners into 2-3 inch pieces.
Make or Print a Butterfly life cycle poster (mine pictured below)

Hang some sort of flower visuals around the room (could be pictures, could be drawings, whatever you have or want to make)

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
I don’t know if other schools do this as well, but in the spring at BCCS all the 3K/Pre-K classes hatch butterflies from caterpillars in their classroom. If classes are doing this, this is a good lesson to do in advance of that.
This lesson could be done in the garden if you have a good space/external noise level for a read aloud and are comfortable doing a simple art project with kids in your garden space.
