Chilled Cucumber and Tahini Soup with Spicy Pumpkin Seeds

(Video may be restricted use)


[Table]
Summary: | Students make a cold cucumber soup using summer vegetables and herbs while learning about Palestinian culture. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will practice kitchen skills such as cutting with a knife, cutting with scissors, and measuring. Students will make a recipe featuring ingredients used in Palestinian cooking.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by deepening students’ knowledge about Palestinian food and ingredients.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students practice self-management by using kitchen tools, such as knives and scissors, in a safe manner. Students practice relationship skills by drawing connections between their own cultural traditions and Palestinian culture.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Students can use late summer produce from the garden (cucumber, tomato, parsley, mint, dill)


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students will be prompted with the question “Do you have a connection to any of the ingredients she mentioned? Do you eat any of these at home with your family?” – inviting self-reflection and a chance to recall other instances where they’ve cooked with these ingredients. Students will be asked to think about how these ingredients can be cross-cultural.


[Table]
Tags: | Palestine, tahini, soup, tomato, cucumber, summer harvest, raw, vegan, blender | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow |  |  | Opening Ritual | (10 minutes) |  | “Today we are making a recipe using some ingredients that grow in our summer garden.” Show cucumber, tomato, and herbs. | “We are also going to be using another ingredient, called sesame.” Pass around cups of sesame seeds at each small group. Ask students to discuss in small groups if they have seen or eaten sesame seeds before. | “Sesame seeds are a very ancient food. People have been eating sesame seeds for thousands of years. Sesame probably first started growing in southern Africa and in India.” Show these places on the map as you go along. “But sesame also became very popular in the Middle East. Sesame plants can grow even in very hot and dry places, even in the desert where very few other plants can grow!” | Show Palestine on the map. “Today we are making a recipe from Palestine, which is a nation in the Middle East. We are going to be combining foods that grow in our garden, like the cucumbers and tomatoes, along with sesame, a traditional Palestinian ingredient, to make a soup.” | “We are going to watch a video about Chef Ayat, who is Palestinian and has her own restaurant in Brooklyn. She’s going to show us how to make one of her favorite dishes.” Watch the | video | from 0:12 to 1:09, then 4:29 to 6:49. Before watching, ask students to watch for what other ingredients Chef Ayat uses in Palestinian cooking. | After watching, write up on the board some of the ingredients that students saw in the video. Have students discuss in small groups or pairs: “Chef Ayat says that Palestinian food is all about bringing family together. Do you have a connection to any of the ingredients she mentioned? Do you eat any of these at home with your family?” |  | Engaging Activity | (30 minutes) | In small groups, students each are assigned a cooking task to prepare for the recipe. Student jobs can include: | Zesting the lemon | Juicing the lemon | Dicing cucumber | Dicing tomato | Measuring tahini | Cutting herbs with scissors | Measuring chili flakes, salt, and pepper | Blend the ingredients, as directed by the recipe. You will likely need to do this in multiple batches. Set aside ingredients for toppings. |  | Closing Ritual | (5 minutes) | Serve soup to students. Allow students to choose their toppings to put on top. Share tasting words and feedback. | “Chef Ayat says that Palestinian food is served in big portions so that lots of people can come together as a family to share. Are there any traditions in your family that you use to bring your family all together?” Have students share in small groups or pairs at their tables. | 

Grade level variations:
For grades PK-1, you can simplify the activity by having the lemon zested ahead of time and/or the lemon squeezed ahead of time. 
For grades 2-5, while the recipe is blending, students can help to set up toppings at their tables. Have students put remaining herbs, tomato, and the pre-roasted pumpkin seeds in small plates or bowls at their table. While waiting, they can set the table with plates and spoons and begin to add toppings to their own soup.
For grades 6-8, students can use an additional sharp (peeler) for cucumbers. Cucumbers and tomatoes may be left whole. Students who demonstrate appropriateness may use the stove to toast/season pumpkin seeds. Students may blend their own soup and check for expected consistency.

Back pocket activity: 
Watch the full video on Palestinian Chef Ayat and her restaurant in Bay Ridge. Have students discuss the following questions:
How was Chef Ayat influenced by her mother to open her restaurant?
Why does Chef Ayat’s husband, Abdul, raise his own animals for the restaurant?
What new things did you learn about Palestine or Palestinian culture from the video?
What other connections did you make to the video?

Compost Relay & Stew



[Table]
Summary: | Students will learn about compost, participate in a compost relay by measuring and adding different ingredients that go into compost, and make a compost “stew” with edible ingredients that represent materials that go into compost. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will understand what happens to their kitchen scraps when they put them in the compost bins. They will recognize that a compost pile needs greens, browns, air and moisture. They will understand compost as a sustainable alternative to trash/landfills and understand why we use compost in the garden to help plants grow. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and group work.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Self-management, relationship skills by taking turns and working in groups, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson teaches a key component of gardening knowledge and skills, compost. This will help students know how to use the compost when they are in the garden - what to put in it, how to take care of it and how to use it to help plants grow. | 


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to think critically about what compost is, what can and cannot go into compost, and think about the ways that they compost or could be composting in different parts of their life. It invites them to work independently and in small groups to make compost as well as an edible representation of compost - this highlights different learning methods and styles and gives students multiple ways to engage and think critically about and understand the purpose of compost.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | Seed starting/making soil, Garden Jobs, Indigenous Peoples Day / Indigenous Heritage Month, Earth Day | Compost, soil, decomposers, food scraps, landfill, worms | Cooking skills: measuring, mixing, Main ingredients: bananas, vanilla extract, milk/milk alternative, coconut flakes, dried figs, pumpkin seeds or pears, Heating element: None


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (5 minutes): | How is everyone feeling today? Show me with your thumbs. |  | Opening Circle | Hello everyone! Today we will be talking about compost. What is compost? What have you learned about compost? Has anyone composted at school, in the garden or at home? What are some things we put in the compost? Point out whether the things they suggest are greens or browns. Show compost cycle | visuals | . What are some things you cannot compost? Why would you compost instead of throwing things in the trash? Emphasize that compost has a lot of nutrients in it that helps our plants grow, how it acts as a fertilizer, how it gives decomposers food and is an important part of the life cycle of our plants and animals. Emphasize that composting is a gardening/land stewardship technique that was learned originally from indigenous people that steward the land. | Watch the | video | . | Ask students, “What are the four main components of compost?” → Greens, browns, air, and moisture. Hold up four signs with the words, greens on green paper, browns on brown paper, air on white paper, moisture on blue paper. | (For 4th-8th Grades) |  | Why do we compost? Where did you learn composting from originally? Emphasize the origin of composting as indigenous knowledge and ask what are other skills we have learned from indigenous land stewards? (companion planting, three sisters, etc.) | Are there landfills in NYC? Where does our waste go? What are the issues transporting waste? Discuss what type of compost we have at school, where does our compost, trash and recycling go? Whose responsibility is it? | NYC calculations waste calculations: | 9 million people x 4 lbs. waste each daily = 36,000,000 lbs. daily | ⅓ of waste is compostable = ~10,000,000 lbs daily |  | Engaging activity (30 minutes total): | (15 minutes) Compost relay - Students are invited to practice their knowledge of compost "ingredients" by participating in a compost relay race. Teams of students work together to add greens, browns and water to their compost bucket, then aerate using a cultivator. | Relay Lesson Plan | . Encourage students to engage in the competition, but to be supportive of each other (how do you encourage your teammates? How can you be supportive of your classmates whether you finish first or do not?) |  | Start With Sticks (on a paper plate) | Scoop Some Soil (in a paper cup) | Add 3 handfuls browns (in a tub trug) | Add 1 handful greens (in a tub trug) | Add 3 handfuls browns (in a tub trug) | Add 1 handful greens (in a tub trug) | Keep It Moist (watering can) | Turn, Turn, Turn (cultivators) | (15 minutes) Compost stew - Make | Banana Nice Cream | compost pile | Start with shredded coconut “sticks” | Scoop some nice cream soil (4 bananas sliced and frozen, ½ tsp vanilla extract, 2 tbsp milk/milk alternative) | Add browns (dried figs) | Add greens (pumpkin seeds or pears) | Layer browns and greens again | Turn with a “cultivator” (fork) |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Tasting - Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks! Taste together. | How do we feel now that class is over? Same, better? What is one thing you learned about compost today? What is one thing you could teach a younger student or a family member about compost? How can you teach them in a kind way (rather than embarrassing or shaming them if they do not know what compost is?) Practice how you would tell someone about what belongs in compost in the cafeteria.

Grade Level Variations:
3K-PK: Pre measure ingredients into different bowls and emphasize their role in pouring in ingredients, mixing together and taking turns.

K-1 and 2-3: Have students work on measuring individually or in groups. 
Emphasize that the measurements are not very strict in this recipe and they can adjust it to taste, add more of something they like more.

3-8: Give students more responsibility in collecting, measuring and adding ingredients. Do demonstrations for chopping up to show them sizes of each thing. Have older students prepare the banana nice cream with blenders if you have extra time and want to teach them this extra skill.

Materials: 
Compost relay
2 empty tub trugs
Sticks on a paper plate
Soil in a paper cup
Browns in a tub trug
Greens in a tub trug
2x Watering can, pre-filled
2x Cultivator
Paper slips with steps, pre-written (below)
Compost stew in individual tasting cups per student
Blue cup of Nice cream “soil” (bananas, vanilla extract, milk/milk alternatives)
Shredded coconut “sticks” 
Chopped fig “browns”
Pumpkin seed “greens” 
Fork per student

Prep work and setup:
Compost relay
Rearrange greenhouse tables and benches
Compost pile steps written on a whiteboard
Pre-cut student relay jobs
Compost stew - prep all ingredients and place into bowls
Make nice cream
Set up tasting cups of nice cream
Set up tasting cups of figs, shredded coconut, pumpkin seeds

Anticipated problems:
There may be an odd or low number of students for the relay. Adjust relay groups accordingly.

Background information for instructor:
Composting at Home
Video: The Perfect Compost Recipe - How to Get Your Compost Heap Cooking!

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Coloring pages, Word searches, Compost critters and other backup activities linked here
Compost Monster worksheet
Compost Stew, Wormy Worm, or Day in the Life of a Worm book
Worm Study lesson plan / observing worms in the worm bin
If you don’t have access to actual compost materials (greens, browns, etc.) or have to do a less messy lesson indoors, alternatively you can use these compost relay visuals or these visuals. Print out and laminate a few copies of each, print out or write “Trash/Landfill,” “Recycling,” and “Compost” on pieces of paper, and have the students take one item (for example: egg carton) and walk from one side of the room to the other to put it in the correct bin. This can be a competition to see who finishes putting all of their items in the correct bins together. At the end, review each item and where the students put it and where it actually belongs. Discuss the nuances of some of the items, for example newspaper can be recycled or put into the compost. For older students, review the different types of compost (backyard/community compost vs. industrial compost, and how things like meat/dairy cannot go into backyard compost but it could go into community compost). Leave time for students to discuss and debate and ask questions about each item, make sure they don’t feel embarrassed for being confused or putting something into the wrong place.

Choose which petal point they are working towards this class.  This lesson especially emphasizes kindness when working in a group and bravery when trying new things.  (15 minutes) |  | Write the word DECOMPOSE up on the white board. Has anyone heard the word decompose before? Raise of hands. | DECOMPOSE means to break down.  It happens in the COMPOST BIN.  Does anyone know what creatures live in the compost bin? | That’s right!  There are lots of worms, roly polys, and other decomposers that live in the compost bin.  They help decompose, or break down food and leaves from the garden. | Hold up a piece of fruit and the compost bin.  If I put this fruit in the compost bin, the roly polys and worms will gobble it up!  It takes a little time, but when they poop it out, it turns into fresh compost that we can put back in the garden to keep our plants healthy and strong. | What things do you know go in the compost? What things do the worms eat? | Hold up a few example items.  Does this decompose?  That’s right, it does!  It will break down in the compost. | Hold up another item–this is too tough for worms to eat!  They can not break plastic or metal down, so it will not decompose. | Explain that we will be starting an experiment to see what items on our trays will decompose and what items will not. | Explain that we will be working in groups during our experiment. Ask students for examples of what good teamwork and sharing will look like when working with their group. |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): |  | Hand out worksheets and read through as a class. Talk about what it means to make a hypothesis or prediction. | A hypothesis is a guess based on facts. | I’ve shown you some things that can break down in the compost–that’s a fact!  Now, you can make a guess about the items on your worksheet.  That’s your hypothesis! | Remind students that they are only filling out the middle column of the experiment sheet–we will fill out the results section during another class. | Have students work in small groups to fill ziploc bags with compost or soil and various items listed on the experiment worksheet. Students can discuss with their group members what items they think will decompose and what items will not. | Circulate and help remind students about the definition of decompose and hypothesis.  Ask students why they think certain items will break down, and certain items will not. | Remind students to put their names on the sheet!  This is important for pt. 2. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Clean up tables and return to seats for hand sanitizer. | Explain to the students that we are going to put these bags in a safe place until we do pt. 2 of this experiment in the spring.  During that class they will get to dig through the bags to see the results. | Highlight student behavior that exemplified good teamwork, such as listening to one another’s ideas, taking turns with the materials, and helping one another to complete tasks. Assign petal points for classes that were particularly kind and/or brave. | Finish with a tasting. Thank you gardeners thank you cooks! |

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: 
This lesson has a lot of new key words, so it may be too advanced for some students’ reading levels.  If you notice this happening in classes, print out page 1 of the canva handout for classes, and focus on teaching students the word decomposition.  
If you want to keep worms and other microbes alive for months between experiments, make sure there is a lot of moist soil in the baggies.  Spray water in bags once a month and zip ¾ shut to allow a little air in.  Keep in a dark, warm place.
It is not necessary to label bags unless you want to!  They should all have identical ingredients in them when you open them up for pt. 2.

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activities–REQUIRED:
If some students finish early, they can look for worms and other microbes in the bags and observe them.
If all students finish early, have table groups walk around the garden and fill a bag with items they think the worms would like to eat if we did this experiment again.  Ask students: how long do you think it would take the worms to decompose all this food?
Hand out copies of the decomposition timeline in a sheet protector.  Ask students: if we put everything back in this bag, and didn’t open it up again until next year, or in 4 years, 10 years, 25 years, what do you think would have decomposed?  Have students make further hypotheses based on the timeline.  
Have students use this worksheet to make and decorate signs of what can and cannot go into the compost, based on the results of their experiment.

Decomposition experiment worksheets (filled out from pt 1)
Older worksheets
Updated worksheet
updated 2025 canva worksheet in english/spanish 
Clipboards
Pencils
Ziploc bags filled with soil and items from decomposition experiment part 1
Popsicle sticks
Hand lenses
Plastic trays to empty the experiment bags into
Optional, 1 per table for backpocket: decomposition timeline 

Prep work and setup:
Set out stations with the Ziploc bags with the decomposition experiment, trays to put it in, hand lenses, and popsicle sticks. 
Put the decomposition worksheets on clipboards. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
This is part 2 in a 2 part series: 
You must allow at least several weeks (and ideally, months!) in between part 1 and part 2 of this lesson series so that the items in the experiment have time to break down and create obvious results for the students to observe. 
It is up to you if you want to have students stay in the same group they were in for pt 1, or break into new groups (experiment will work the same either way).  
Some organic materials may have only partially decomposed, or not have decomposed at all depending on how much time has passed since pt 1 of the experiment.  Explain to them that some materials take a lot longer than others to break down.  Show students a copy of this decomposition timeline and accompanying backpocket activity listed above.

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

If done with younger grades you can pre measure ingredients if desired (I did it with 1st-5th and it was entertaining the whole time for them)

2-3: Focus on Dr. Carver as a scientist/innovator and how he thought critically about how to use plants for medicinal purposes/oils/other functional purposes. Ask them who they would like to make this for/how they would use it. Also you can lean into the artistic decoration part of the lesson as a part of innovation too. 

4-5: Encourage students to think critically about how the medicines/pharmaceutical industry makes us feel far away from the natural world and the origins of these medicines. Discuss why it is important to learn about herbalism and what plants have medicinal properties. How can we be innovators like Dr. Carver? What plants in our garden could be used to make a salve? What would that salve be used for? Have students write out recipes for other salves with herbs from our garden, draw them too!

Pesticides



[Table]
Summary: | Students will engage in a food web activity to understand how pesticide use affects many species and then discuss what some alternatives to pesticide use are. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will understand that pesticide use is harmful to the land, pollinators, food plants, farm workers, and more.


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson positions the teacher as facilitator by having students direct themselves and participate in physical movement to learn new information.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students practice social awareness by learning about pesticides and their impact on ecosystems. Students also practice relationship skills by working together to build the food web during the activity.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Students recognize that our garden has a lot of biodiversity. Students understand that we grow specific plants in our gardens to attract pollinators. Students will make a food web for the garden ecosystem. Students understand that The Three Sisters planting method is an example of companion planting.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students are encouraged to consider the impact of monoculture farming.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | food web, pesticide, pollinators, farm workers, crops



[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Start by asking students what they discussed last time (biodiversity and monoculture). Have them explain why monoculture is harmful to pollinators. If the previous lesson in the unit was not taught, or if review would be helpful, explain the concepts of diversity and monoculture with | pictures | . | Remind students that a garden with many types of plants is better for pollinators, and we need pollinators in order to have gardens and food. | Biodiversity: having lots of different kinds of plants, animals, and bugs all living in one area. They each function as one big team where each member has a specific role. | Monoculture: when there is only one kind of plant growing in a large area - it’s like having a soccer team with only goalies. | Explain that we are going to keep learning about bees and pollinators so that we can be good caretakers of our garden and our environment. | “Today, I want to talk about another farming method that hurts bees, farmers, and plants. Does anyone know what pesticides are?” Explain that pesticides are harmful chemicals that, when used on a farm, make the animals and land sick. | Why are pesticides used? | To keep away bugs and disease, prevent weeds, and to create more crops and more store-quality crops. Some bugs, like ladybugs and bees, are helpful to plants, but others are harmful and eat plants. Ask students to give examples of bugs that might eat plants (locusts, aphids, leaf beetles). | “Pesticides are very dangerous for all bugs and animals. So it will kill pests but also animals that are really important to the farm and food. Can anyone guess what animal that might be? Right! Bees!” | “Even if you are a farmer that does not use pesticides, if your neighbor does, it will impact your farm. Or, if you are a farmer and decide to stop using pesticides, it will take more than five years for the chemicals to leave your farm!” | “Farmers who work in the fields are especially impacted. We’ll talk more about this next class, so keep it in mind.” |  | Engaging activity: |  | “We are going to do an activity that shows us how a pesticide on one animal can impact so much more than that one animal!” Ask students what they already know about a food web. | Explain that in a food web, we make connections based on who is eating what and who is getting eaten. Ask students to think of an example of something in our garden that gets eaten, or something in our garden that eats something else. | Use the white board to draw an example of a simple food web. Distinguish between a food web and a food chain. | Ask students to remind you: “Since plants can’t eat, where do they get their energy?” Emphasize that the sun is actually the beginning of any food chain. | Split the class in half for the activity. | Give each student a food chain card necklace. Have students look at their cards. Explain that this is who they will represent in the food chain. Ask students to read who eats them and who they eat, then to put on the necklace when they are finished reading. | Model with another student how to read what is on the card and then decide on who else in the food web they are connected to. Before playing the game, students can walk around and talk to each other about their role in the food chain. | Model rolling the ball of yarn across to make that connection. Model how to hold the string and then pass it to the next student in the food web. | Start with one student, holding the string. Have the student name another animal or plant that they are connected to. Unwind the string and connect the next student, starting to form the web. Facilitate moving the ball of string in the group until all students are included in the web. | Now that the food web is complete, demonstrate what happens when a pesticide is introduced. Have the affected animals drop their section of the string, then have students who were connected to those animals drop their strings, until the entire web has fallen apart. “Even if just one or a few part(s) of the web are impacted, it hurts all animals, plants, and people involved.” |  | Closing ritual: |  | “Learning about pesticides can be very overwhelming! It’s scary to know that the bees (and other animals) are in danger.” | “We’ve already learned about some solutions to help with this problem, though!” | “Last week we talked about biodiversity. Biodiversity creates natural solutions, like companion planting and pest management.” | Give an example of tomato plants and marigold, ladybugs and aphids, or The Three Sisters. | “Community gardens like ours are another great solution! We grow lots of delicious foods without pesticides. We learn in our tastings at the end of class, sometimes the food that grows in the garden doesn’t look perfect, but that doesn’t mean we can’t eat it, or it tastes bad!” | Finish with a seasonal tasting. |

Agenda/Class Flow: ( | 90 minutes) |  | Opening ritual (15 minutes): | Welcome students back to the kitchen. Remind students of our safety norms: | Touch only your own tools and ingredients. | Always walk in the kitchen classroom. | Use a bear claw and a sawing motion when cutting. | Use all tools only for their intended job, not for playing. | Be responsible for cleaning up your own space. | Wait for everyone to eat all together. | Stay away from the oven. It will be on during class today! | Express your opinions about the food using respectful language; don’t yuck anyone else’s yum. | Keep an open mind and try new things! | Explain that today we will be making a dish called empanadas. Show the map on the board. Explain that empanadas originally came from Spain, but are now eaten all over Latin America, in countries like Mexico, Argentina, Colombia, Venezuela, Peru, and Chile. Different countries make empanadas in different styles with different fillings, and they may have slightly different names.  For example, in Brazil they are called | pasteles | and in the Dominical Republic they are called | pastelitos. | Empanadas can be sweet or savory. | We are also making a corn salad today to go with our empanadas. This salad isn’t traditional to Latin America, but it uses ingredients like cilantro, corn, peppers, and tomatoes that are important in Mexican and Central American cooking. |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): | Sanitize hands before beginning cooking. | Give each student a task list of the steps to make the empanadas, or give oral instructions for students who are not yet reading. | Pass out tools and ingredients to each student. | Rotate to assist students as needed. | When students are finished with the first part of the recipe, collect the empanadas on a tray. Put them in the oven to bake. | Collect unneeded tools and materials from the first component of the lesson. |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): | While the empanadas are baking, students can make their corn salad. Show students the second page of their task card, or give oral instructions for students who are not yet reading. | Pass out additional tools and materials needed for the corn salad. | Rotate to assist students as needed. | While students are finishing up, remove the empanadas from the oven to cool. |  | Closing ritual (25 minutes): | While students are cleaning up, circulate to give them back their baked empanadas. Put it on the plate where they made the corn salad. | Students eat all together and share tasting words and feedback. Ask students to share how they feel after having made two recipes today independently. | Clean up plates, silverware, and cups. | 

Grade Level Variations – see anticipated problems for more grade level variations
3k-Pk: Verbal instructions. Pre-measure ingredients, let them chop 1-2 easy veggies (scallion and sweet potato), pre-measure the corn salad ingredients and have them focus on mixing. Can squeeze lime in citrus reamer with teacher assistance.
You can have pre-K students do a modified, 45-minute version of this lesson just making the corn salad. They can eat it with tortilla chips.  
K-1: Verbal instructions. Pre-measure ingredients, let them chop all ingredients for empanadas. Use only scissors and cut scallion and cilantro for corn salad, all other ingredients are pre-chopped and measured. Focus on mixing and adding ingredients. 
2-3: Give verbal instructions to 2nd and task sheets to 3rd. Can do almost anything 4-5 graders can do, depending on what point in the year you are doing this recipe. 2nd graders may need to have some ingredients pre-chopped and measured to save time. 
4-5: Use task sheets and students can work independently


Back pocket activities: 
Print out visuals from the Dumplings Around the World document. Have students take turns at their table looking at the pictures and reading about each kind of food. Students can either share about their food with their tablemates, or they can pass the visuals around so they can see all the kinds of dumplings from around the world. Students in grades 1-2 can do the worksheet to draw and write about foods they eat at home. 
For kindergarten, read the book Round Is A Tortilla by Roseanne Thong about the different shaped foods we might eat at home. Students can draw foods of different shapes on the accompanying worksheet.
Older students can create their own cookbooks. Students can decorate the covers and copy in or paste in recipe cards. Template is available here. Have colored pencils, stickers, old recipes, and cookbooks available for each table to decorate their book and get inspiration.

Materials: 
World map
Ingredients: frozen corn, bell peppers, tomatoes, limes, scallion, cilantro, pre-cooked sweet potatoes, olive oil, black beans, salt, cumin, black pepper, empanada wrappers
Plate
Scissors 
Safety knives
Cutting boards
Forks
Task sheets, in plastic sleeves
Sheet pans for pre roasting the sweet potatoes
Parchment paper to cook empanadas on
Oven
Cleaning supplies
Colander for rinsing black beans
Take home Recipes

Prep work and setup:
Set up a compost bin in the center of each table for food scraps and a bottle of hand sanitizer. 
Cut sweet potatoes into strips and roast in the oven with olive oil, salt, garlic powder, and chili powder at 425 Fahrenheit until soft.
Rinse black beans and thaw frozen corn.
Pre-cut tomatoes and peppers into strips. 
Pre-heat oven to 400 Fahrenheit for baking empanadas during class. 
Arrange materials for each table, with one per student of each of the following:
Scissors
Safety knife
Cutting board
Fork
Task sheet, laminated in plastic
Plate with ingredients for empanada filling
Empanada wrapper
One small bucket per table for compost and one hand sanitizer
Set up the table for empanada assembly first, and have corn salad materials and ingredients set up on the side to be passed out once empanadas are in the oven.
For younger grades, only set tables up with cutting boards. Pass out materials and ingredients in sequence as they are needed. It may be hard for younger students to listen to directions if all the materials and ingredients are in front of them.

Anticipated problems:
To keep track of which empanadas belong to which students, write the students’ names or their tables and seat numbers on a parchment paper that sits under the empanadas on the tray. If possible, do this before class. 
Older students may be able to work through the steps independently using the task sheet. Set up for each student a plate with pre-measured beans, a wedge of sweet potato, lime, and scallion. Students can start their empanada assembly independently, with the teacher circulating to help students as needed. Pass out the empanada wrappers once all other steps are done.
Once empanadas are collected, measure out corn onto the plates that students are already using to reduce the number of used. Pass out other ingredients and let students work independently on their corn salad. 
Younger students will need to be given oral instructions in smaller chunks to make it easy to follow and may be unable to use the task cards. The following is a suggested order of materials and tools for grades K-3:
Give out the fork and plate with beans and sweet potatoes, with garlic, salt, and cumin already on the ingredients. Have students cut the sweet potato into cubes using their safety knife. Then, students can mash sweet potatoes and beans on their plate. Have students use scissors to cut scallions and add them to their plate. Give them a wedge of lime to squeeze onto their filling. Mix well together. 
Lay out the empanadas wrapper on a cutting board for students. Show them how to scoop the filling in, then fold. Emphasize that the filling needs to be in a small circle in the center of the wrapper. Show them how to use their fork to crimp the edge.
Take away empanadas to bake. 
Give students scissors and pre-measured corn. Have them cut their cilantro and scallions using the scissors into the plate with the corn. Time permitting, give each student a tablespoon and allow them to measure 3-4 scoops of corn into their own plate or container.
Collect the scissors and give out safety knives. Have students cut the tomato and pepper and add it to the plate.
Collect knives. Give out limes. Have students squeeze lime juice over their salad. Use the fork they already have to mix. 
Students may finish cooking at different times. For students who finish early, give them other tasks to help with, such as cleaning tables, sweeping floors, emptying compost buckets, or collecting tools. You can assign each seat at each table a number corresponding with each job. 

Background information for instructor:
Each student should receive the following quantities of the ingredients for their individual recipe:
Corn salad:
¼ cup corn
1/12 bell pepper (one strip)
¼ tomato
⅛ lime
Sprig of cilantro
Small piece of scallion
Empanada:
1 TBSP beans
1 large strip of sweet potato or 2 small ones, approx 35 g
Small piece of scallion
¼ lime

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Prep work and setup:
Assess what garden jobs need to be done and how many students can safely and effectively participate in the job. 
Set up tools and materials for the number of students.
Prepare and clear the area where students will be working, checking for any potential dangers or distractions. 

Anticipated problems:
Each garden job presents its own specific challenges; more detail is given in the background information section below. 
Scaffold jobs based on your students’ abilities and personalities, their prior experience, and their age. For instance, older students may be able to plant seeds or bulbs independently, but younger students may need step-by-step guidance through each part of the process. 
Like all of us, students are liable to make errors, especially if it's their first time doing a particular job! Be willing to accept students’ mistakes and learning as part of the process during the garden job. If you really need a job to be done without error,  leave it for another adult or teacher to do.

Building Compost
Clearing
Harvesting
Mulching
Planting Seeds
Planting Starts
Saving Seeds
Seed Starting
Sifting Compost
Turning Compost
Watering
Weeding

Summary: | Students will get a (re)introduction to the school garden and learn about the various ways gardening is beneficial for the environment. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to explore their school garden to experience and reflect on the benefits of gardening on a community and environmental level.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Garden skills and related academic content, Social-emotional learning, Environmental and Community Stewardship


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Communicates high expectations: | Conveying to students your faith in their abilities, Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame | Reshapes curriculum: | incorporating movement, Incorporating group work | 


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | Self-management: | Take small risks in using new tools. | Relationship skills: | Working well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants | Responsible decision-making: | Putting materials back where they belong, Using tools safely | Self-awareness: | Display a verbal or physical action of joy | 


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Students will explore their school garden and participate in a supported garden job.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students will be prompted to think about how they relate to the world around them – they will contemplate how gardening affects the environment and identify its benefits. They will use prior and outside knowledge to connect garden concepts to the world they live in.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : Garden, garden jobs, urban gardening, SEL, socioemotional health | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow |  | Opening Ritual | (10 minutes) | Welcome to the Garden (or greenhouse/classroom)! Does anyone remember what are the main things we do during class here? Sometimes we will cook and other times we will head outside to our school garden. Today we will be talking more about gardening. Gardening can impact us in many ways, in smaller ways on an individual level but also in much bigger ways - like the entire planet. What are some ways you think gardening helps our environment and our planet? | improves the quality of air and soil | decreases carbon dioxide in the air to reduce effect of global warming | replenishes and protects the soil | protects local wildlife and pollinators | Makes the food system more local by growing our own food | reduces "heat islands" and make our cities cooler in the summer | Beautifies our planet |  | Today you’ll be doing your part to help the environment by helping our school garden. |  | Engaging Activity | (25 minutes) |  | Go over garden rules: | Stay in the garden area | No running in the garden | Don’t pick/eat anything without permission | No throwing dirt or watering our friends. | Try new things & have fun!) |  | Garden Job Review: As a class let’s review each garden job | ( | ask for volunteers to describe each job) | Planting: putting seeds or seedlings (young plants) in the ground to grow | Weeding: removing any unwanted or troublesome plant | Harvesting: the process of gathering ripe crops | Composting: recycling leaves and food scraps, into fertilizer for soil and plants | Watering: adding water to plants so that they can grow |  | Now we are going to go out to the garden! There are stations throughout the garden where you will do each of the jobs that we just learned about. Try your best to make it to every station. | Garden jobs. |  |  | Closing Ritual | (10 minutes) | Say thank yous and eat a tasting together as a class community | Go around the room and have each student share 1 word about describing how they felt working in the garden (use | mood meter | if available) | 

Grade Level Variations–

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: Garden Alphabet Game or another Scavenger Hunt

Group Game (best for smaller classes, ~10 students who have had experience in the garden):
One person is “it”. The rest of the group gathers and sits in one spot.
When the “it” person is turned away, the teacher will hold up a sign with a plant growing in the garden and silently tell the group where it is, specifically. Wait for all students to silently nod and confirm they understand the chosen plant.
From the group, one person starts by giving a clue on where the plant is.
The student who is “it” will start walking over to what they think it is. The group is allowed to say “hot” if they are close, and “cold” if they are far.
Next person in the group gives a clue and the “it” person will again move to where they think the plant is. Keep doing this until the “it” student locates the plant. Switch out who’s “it” next

Gingerbread Cookies


[Table]
Summary: |  | Students will practice baking skills to make gingerbread cookies and share about how they celebrate the holidays. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to read and follow a baking recipe, discuss holiday food traditions, celebrate with seasonal flavors. | 


[Table]
Core Competencies: | List all that apply: kitchen skills, cultural diversity


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness: | promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Honey is an ingredient in this recipe and is produced by an important pollinator in our garden - bees!


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to practice measuring and allows for independence by exploring what flavors and tastes they like. | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Ask students if they know what a tradition is. Ask if anyone would like to share some of their family traditions for the holidays | Introduce gingerbread cookies as a holiday tradition | Introduce ingredients, pass around whole cinnamon sticks and ginger to show where spices come from, define molasses | Before telling students more about molasses, have them each taste a little bit. Encourage them to make their own observations. How does it taste? Smell? What color is it? What is the texture? | Molasses: a thick, dark syrup that comes from sugar cane (the same plant that white sugar comes from!). It’s kind of like honey but not as sweet, and it’s sticky and slow when it pours. | Pass out group recipes and have students read through them together | Recap recipe as a class, review measuring tools and demonstrate rolling dough | Groups choose roles, transition and clean hands |  | Engaging activity: | Groups add all pre-measured ingredients to a bowl and take turns mixing. When ready, they can measure out flour to add to the bowl and mix again. | Once dough is ready, students scoop out 1 tbsp and roll into a ball, flatten, and cut with cookie cutters (optional) | Teachers collect cookies to put on baking sheet with parchment | While baking, groups clean up, wash hands if needed, and complete | My Holiday Tradition | worksheet. |  | Closing ritual: | Taste it together | Vote tried/liked/loved & have students give feedback on how it tastes | Presenters share how it was baking as a team | Have students pair-share about their holiday celebrations, then call on some to share with the class


[Table]
Ingredients |  | 1 egg (or flax egg or applesauce) | ⅓ cup oil | ⅓ cup honey | ¼ cup molasses | ½ tsp vanilla extract | 1 tsp dried ginger, ground | ½ tsp cinnamon | ½ tsp nutmeg | ½ tsp baking soda | ¼ tsp salt | 1 ¼ cups All Purpose flour | 1 cup Whole Wheat flour |  | Steps |  | Preheat oven to 350 degrees F. | Add all of the ingredients | except for flour | to a bowl and mix until combined. | Add the flour, mixing with a spatula and then with your hands. | Transfer dough to a lightly floured surface | . |  | Lightly flour the rolling pin and roll the dough to 1/4 inch thick. Cut into shapes and place on a baking tray lined with parchment paper. | Bake for 10-12 minutes until golden brown. Remove from the oven and let cool completely.








Grade Level Variations:
3k-PK: Before cooking, introduce students to the ingredients. Have students repeat the name of ingredients back to you. Encourage them to make observations using smell and sight. What color is each ingredient? Does it smell good? Thumbs up or thumbs down. For the baking portion, ingredients should be pre-measured and placed in smaller bowls. With help, students can add dry ingredients to the bowl and mix. Students can then add wet ingredients to the bowl and mix. Before mixing all the ingredients, students can practice mixing using empty bowls and a whisk. The teacher has the option to pre-roll the dough and have students use cookie cutters or students can roll the dough with assistance. Students can also flatten the dough using their hands rather than a rolling pin. Beforehand students could practice this using play dough. 
K - 1st: All students should be introduced to the ingredients. The teacher can hold up the ingredients and have the students identify them. Encourage students to make observations about unfamiliar ingredients.  For the kindergarteners, ingredients should be pre-measured in smaller bowls. 1st graders can measure ingredients themselves. 
2nd - 3rd: These grades can complete the lesson as written. 
4th - 5th: These grades can complete the lesson as written. 


Back Pocket Activity: 
Play holiday music, add hot cider or cocoa as a drink
Gingerbread Cookies Coloring Sheet 

Materials: 
Tray Prep List
Mixing bowl & mixing spoon
Flour
Applesauce egg 
Pre-measured oil, honey, molasses
Pincher cups: nutmeg/ginger/cinnamon, baking soda/salt, vanilla, salt
Baking sheets with parchment paper
Cookie cutters
Bowl of flour for rolling dough
Cutting boards (share with partners)

Prep work and setup:
Premake a batch of cookies and a batch of dough
Print group recipe task card, Take-home recipes, and holiday traditions half-sheet. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems:
Recipe - https://ifoodreal.com/healthy-gingerbread-cookies-recipe/ 
Flour might get everywhere, allow time to wash hands after

Mobile: Green “Acai” Bowls (without Acai)


[Table]
Summary: | Students will learn how to make a healthy breakfast, snack or dessert–a smoothie bowl in the style of an acai bowl. They will learn about the Brazilian origins of acai. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be excited about and know how to make a healthy breakfast, snack or dessert. They will get creative decorating their acai bowls. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness: | refers to indigenous Brazilian culture


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | self-management, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Welcome students. (5 minutes) | Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools. | Ask if anyone has ever had a smoothie. If yes, what kinds of smoothies do they like? | Has anyone ever had a smoothie bowl? What about an acai (pronounced Ah-sah-ee) bowl? | Use | these pictures | to talk about the origins of acai bowls. | Acai is from the Brazilian rain forest. | It’s a berry that grows on palm trees. | In Brazil, the traditional way to eat acai is in a warm and salty puree. | But in the United States, it’s become trendy to make cold, sweet acai bowls with fruity toppings. | Acai is delicious and it’s good for you. But it needs to grow in a tropical place, so we can’t grow it here in New York. It can also be hard to find in a grocery store and it can be expensive. Thankfully, blueberries are a good substitute–they are a similar color and have similar vitamins and nutrients and they are also delicious, so we are going to use them today. | We’re also going to do something a little crazy–we’re going to make our smoothie bowls green! | Green smoothies and green smoothie bowls are kind of magical. You are eating a lot of vegetables–today it will be spinach–but all you are tasting are the sweet fruits that you put in the smoothie. And you get the nutrition from all of it–the fruits and the vegetables. | It’s always healthy to eat fruits and vegetables, and if you eat different colors of fruits and vegetables, you’ll get different health benefits. The health benefits that come from the green leafy vegetables like spinach are iron, which is good for your blood, and calcium, which is good for your bones. |  | Engaging activity: Make smoothies bowls | Show | pictures | of smoothie bowls and have students look at the trays of ingredients and name the pens they can name. | Safety and Materials (5 minutes | Do a demo of handling knives and cutting. | Go over safety and the materials we will be using. | Pass out cutting boards and knives. | Pass out | task cards |  | Explain that students will be chopping the fruits they will use as their toppings while the teacher uses the blender to make the smoothie. The teacher will have the ingredients that go into the blender at the front of the room: some of the bananas, the frozen blueberries, the orange juice and the chia seeds. | Before handing out the topping ingredients, show each of the ingredients that will go into the blender to make the smoothie. | Pass out topping ingredients: kiwi, strawberries and bananas, and show the students how to cut them. You can model different ways to cut the fruit–sliced, diced, chopped–because students can choose how they want their toppings to look. | You can either give each student the fruit for their own toppings and have them keep it on their own cutting boards. Or you can have them work as a group and put the toppings into bowls which they will then share. | Explain that while students are chopping their toppings, you will call a few people up to the front of the room one at a time to add the smoothie ingredients to the blender. | Make batches of smoothie and spoon it into small serving bowls as you go. | Get students to help pass out the bowls once they are all ready–this will take at least a few batches with the blender. | Once everyone has their smoothies, hand out the remaining topping–coconut flakes. | Students can start adding their toppings. Encourage students to consider how the smoothie bowls will look and taste and to be creative and artistic with their toppings. Remind them not to eat until you tell them it’s time! |  | Closing ritual: | Once the students are done decorating their smoothie bowls: |  | If there is time, you can have them show everyone their beautiful bowls or you can walk around admiring them. | Before the t students taste their creations, have them say “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks” | Hand out spoons and tell students to begin eating. | As they are eating smoothies, ask students to describe what they’re tasting. | . |

Growing Indoor Edible Sprouts 
Note: This is the first of three lessons, taking place on day one. Lesson two should be done on day five. Lesson three should be done on day ten. Sprouts need to be watered once a day between days five and ten. Teacher instructions here and here.


[Table]
Summary: | Students plant “soil sprouts” as a first step toward growing microgreens for their seed-to-table salad unit. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to plant seeds and know what plants need to survive.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, Garden skills and related academic content.


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | Promotes student-centered instruction by giving students a hands-on way to engage with life sciences.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students will practice self-management by learning a new garden skill and being careful with their planting supplies.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson gives students a way to garden indoors when they cannot access the garden space, and nurture sprouts that can either be harvested or transplanted outdoors in another season.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson challenges students to plant seeds independently and think critically about what a plant needs to survive.


[Table]
Tags: |  | Garden Jobs, Seed starting, Making Soil | S | oil, Seeds, Planting, Harvest, Sprouts, Water, Stewardship


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: |  | Opening ritual (5 minutes): | Welcome students to class. “Even though we are indoors, we can still do some gardening today. Does anyone have plants at home that grow indoors? What do they need to be taken care of?” Give students time to respond. | Tell students that they will be doing a special kind of planting today. This is a way to grow seeds quickly, in a small indoor space! You can go over all the steps ahead of time, or go through each step together as you do the planting. |  | Tell students that this is a way they can be gardeners, even if they don’t have any outdoor space to garden. | Tell students that they are going to grow and harvest sprouts that they will eat in a salad. They will be creating a salad from seed to table! |  | Engaging activity (35 minutes): | Explain to the students that the first step will be to put compost in the bottom of their pan. This will help their plants to grow strong. Each tray should get 1 tablespoon of compost, or a heaping spoonful. | Then the pan gets a layer of kelp in the bottom of the tray. The kelp helps the plant to grow up quickly. Each pan should get ½ teaspoon of kelp, or a scant spoonful. | Then the pan gets filled up almost to the top with the potting mix. Tell the students to be careful and not mix up their layers! They are layering all the good nutrition at the bottom, so the seeds will have to grow deep roots to get to the nutrition. | The next thing is to spread the seeds out on top. Tell the students that today they should not cover their seeds up! This is from the usual way of planting, but today they are going to cover them up with something else. | Before the students cover the seeds, have them complete “Day One” of their | Soil Sprouts Observation Sheet. |  | After students complete their observations, they will cover their seeds with wet newspaper. Students can use spray bottles to dampen the newspaper, then cover the trays. The newspaper will keep the seeds damp. | Once the pans are prepared, they should be kept away from light for four days. They will start to sprout and push up the newspaper. After that, the newspaper can be removed and the pans can be placed by a window for light. After about 10 days, the sprouts will be tall enough to eat! |  | Closing ritual (5 minutes): | Tell students they took the first steps toward growing their own food and that they made scientific predictions. | Ask students to share some of their predictions for what they will find on day five.

Grade Level Variations:
Older students can work together in pairs or groups. Younger students may need one on one help with planting.

Back Pocket Activity: 
Plant part scavenger hunt lesson worksheets
Plant part salad coloring sheets
Activities in plant parts work book
Plant Part Simon Says 
Determine with the class a pose for each part of the plant (seeds, roots, stem, leaves, flower, and fruit)
Play Simon Says! The teacher can lead the first round and then you can have a student be Simon in later rounds. 

Materials: 
Small aluminum loaf pans–one per student
Spoons–one per student or they can share
Spray bottles–a few that can be shared.
Kelp
Compost
Potting mix 
½ teaspoon and tablespoon measures, or plastic spoons
Seeds for sprouting (such as peas or sunflower seeds), soaked
Newspaper
Spray bottles
Scissors
Soil Sprouts Observation Sheet
Pencils/colored pencils

Prep work and setup:
Soak seeds overnight, about one TB seeds per loaf pan.
If you don’t want students to cut newspaper, cut it in advance to fit on top of loaf pans

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Potting mix can be made by mixing 6 parts coconut coir to 1 part vermiculite and 1 part perlite. If you want to use pre-made potting mix, be sure that it doesn’t include fertilizers or silicon pellets that retain water. 
If students have allergies, kelp can be omitted. Including kelp, if you are able, will help the sprouts to grow more quickly.

[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (10 minutes): | The teacher explains that today’s class will be about Native Americans, or Indigenous People. What have you all learned about Indigenous People? Does anyone know what indigenous people in New York are called? Lenape! | Explain that thousands of years ago, New York City looked very different. “There were no big buildings or subways or parks, but instead hills and forests. The land of New York City belonged to the Lenni Lenape people, and for thousands of years they cared for and honored this land.” Show | image | (p.1) from the Welikia Project. Ask students for reactions and observations. | “All across North America there were other tribes and Nations of Indigenous people. Today I want to talk about the Haudenosaunee People, an alliance of six tribes. The Haudenosaunee People’s traditional lands are in other parts of New York State.” Show | image | (p.2) | of map of New York or the image from p.17 of | this reading | . “Each tribe has their own language and culture, but all live by The Great Law of Peace. This tells people how to treat others, the world, and how to live in community together.” | “Every year, many Americans celebrate Thanksgiving and take time to share what they are thankful for. The Haudenosaunee People practiced gratitude everyday, not just on Thanksgiving, through their Thanksgiving Address. This address tells us to appreciate and acknowledge all things.” Today we’re going to practice gratitude in class. Can anyone share what you are grateful for? | Before reading, explain that Native American people understand that people and nature have an “ | interconnected” | relationship. In this understanding, the land provides for people, and people have a responsibility to care for the land in return. | Read the | reading | . | Discussion questions/prompts for after the reading: | What was your favorite part? | What parts did you connect with most? | Why is it important to be thankful for nature’s gifts? | Do you think all people see themselves as interconnected with nature? Why or why not? |  |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Lead students in a seasonal garden job. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | At the end, have students partake in their own gratitude activity, sharing something in the natural world that they are thankful for and how they can take care of that thing.

Anticipated problems:
Students may come with preconceived notions about Indigenous Peoples, the history behind Thanksgiving, and our general right to live on this land. Correct students and inform them of the truthful history and help them respect and appreciate Indigenous knowledge and tradition.

Background information for instructor:
A reading from the Museum of the American Indian provides much more context on the Haudenosaunee people and their lives.
An interactive map details ancestral homelands of different tribes and nations.

Photos: 
From the Welikia Project
Map of New York state that shows different tribal lands
Document with the two images to print before class

Back pocket activities: 
Garden gratitude meditation. “Everyone close your eyes. Take a deep breath in and a deep breath out. What do you smell? What do you hear? What do you feel? Think about your favorite part of the garden, maybe it’s a flower or a bench or a garden bed. Let’s thank the flowers for growing, the sun for shining, the butterflies for pollinating, etc. Thank you garden.”

For older students: Read and learn more about the indigenous people native to NYC, the Lenape.

Healing with Roses(Instructions and article may be restricted use)


[Table]
Summary: | Students will | make a rose glycerite tincture (glycerite is a sweet syrupy plant-based extract) and rose infused honey to understand the healing properties of roses, physically, emotionally and spiritually. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to recognize the healing elements of roses through making two rose based solutions from rose petals/rose hips from their garden.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, kitchen skills and related academic content, social justice


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson reshapes the curriculum by introducing ancient practices of using flowers and plants as healing remedies that have been practiced by herbalists globally throughout history.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students will practice social awareness skills, by learning about ways to take care of themselves and their communities through herbal remedies. They will be introduced to social justice issues surrounding the American healthcare system, and learn about community herbalism as an act of self and community autonomy and care.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson gives students an opportunity to use rose petals/rose hips that they have harvested in their garden and dried in the classroom. They will learn about the process of preserving and drying flowers seasonally to be used in food, drinks, and recipes.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to think critically about the US healthcare system while considering alternatives to Western Medicine. Students will learn there are many ways to heal themselves and their communities by using plants!


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : Fall garden jobs, Valentine’s day, could be adapted to a specific region of the world or heritage month (roses are significant in most regions and cultures) | If cooking | : Rose petals, rose hips, vegetable glycerite, honey, measuring & mixing, no heat | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: (10 min) |  | Welcome students back to garden and kitchen class and review class expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” |  | “Raise your hands if you have heard the word herbalism. Does anyone want to tell us what you know about herbalism? Does anyone/anyone’s family members practice herbalism?” | Let a few students share. Tell them that herbalism is when people use plants for medicinal or therapeutic purposes, often as an alternative to Western medicine (what we use in the American healthcare system - the medicine we get when we go to the doctor). Our healthcare system is very inaccessible to many people - it is very expensive and many people do not have health insurance and can’t get medicine that they need. | “Today we are going to be practicing herbalism, by making a tincture and an infused honey from the rose petals/hips that we grew in the garden, harvested and dried. Roses are used for healing in many cultures all over the world. People have used roses for physical healing - to treat colds, flu, body pain, cool down the body from fevers and inflammation, and for emotional healing - depression, anxiety and grief. There are many herbalist practices and recipes that have been passed down- like making rose water, rose syrup, rose tea, rose body lotion or salves, but today we are going to make two special ones: a rose glycerite tincture & rose infused honey. You will be able to take these home and use them in whatever way you choose to - for healing, in your breakfast, etc.” | Ask if anyone has questions or extra knowledge about roses or herbalism to share. |  | Engaging activity: (25 min) | “We are going to make rose glycerite tinctures, which taste sweet, and you can take it when you are feeling sick, sad, low energy, dehydrated, or really any time. You could take it every morning like medicine if you want. You will each get a tincture dropper bottle and each table will get vegetable glycerin and rose petals/rose hips and you will mix them together to create a healing rose tincture. | Do a demonstration of making a rose tincture. (If roses are fresh, add enough glycerin to fully cover the plant material and fill the jar within one inch of the top. For dried roses, dilute glycerin with distilled water in a 3:1 ratio, 3 parts glycerin to one part water) and fill the jar with mixture to within one inch of the top.” | Pass out tincture dropper bottles, glycerite, measuring cups, stirring sticks, and rose petals/hips and | instructions | for how to make a glycerite w/cup measurements. | Tell students to measure and mix their tinctures. | “Now we are going to make rose infused honey. You can eat a little bit when you’re sick or put it in your tea. You will each get a small mason jar and each table will get honey and rose petals/rose hips and you will mix them together to create rose infused honey. After we mix in the rose, we have to let it sit for a few weeks before we can use it.” | Do a demonstration of making rose honey. | Pass out mason jars, honey, stirring sticks, and rose petals/hips. | Tell students to measure and mix their rose honey. |  | Closing ritual with SEL component: (5 min) | “How will you all use your rose tincture/rose honey? Turn and talk with a partner and tell them what you will or would like to use this for.

Grade Level Variations: 
For younger students, pre measure glycerine/honey/rose petals/rose hips. Additionally, you may also simplify the launch. Ask, “what do we use plants for?” Tell students that, in addition to food, plants can be used to make medicine. “In your family, do you eat certain foods when you’re feeling sick to help you feel better?”. Students share. Teachers share that these are all examples of using plants as medicine! 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Make Herbal Honey with other herbs from the garden
Article about the symbolism of roses in different cultures and traditions & discussion
Article about rose water & discussion
Watch rose profile video & draw roses
Rose coloring sheet

Materials: 
Glycerine
Rose petals
Rose hips
Honey
Tincture dropper bottles
Small mason jars

Prep work and setup:
Prep rose glycerite tincture / rose honey materials by table group

Hoppin' John Burgers




[Table]
Summary: |  | Students will learn about ingredients as they relate to African American history then use those ingredients to make Hoppin’ John burgers. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to make a twist on a traditional African American dish and learn about the history of its ingredients.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | kitchen skills and related academic content, cultural diversity


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness: | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by focusing on African American history. This lesson also reframes the curriculum by highlighting the importance of African American food traditions in American food.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | This lesson has students practice social awareness in learning more deeply about injustice against African American people during American history, while recognizing the central role that African Americans have had in shaping American food culture.


[Table]
Garden Connection: How does this lesson make use of the garden? N/A


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? Students each complete their cooking tasks using recipe/task cards.


[Table]
Tags: | For all lessons: | African American Heritage Month, Southern Cooking |  | If cooking: | chopping, mashing,  black eyed peas, rice, scallion, sweet potato; Heating element: stove


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.(15 minutes): |  | Welcome students. Today, we are going to be learning about a dish that was invented in America by African American farmers and cooks. People who are African American have heritage from the continent of Africa. In the Introduction Slides, show the world map and highlight the continent of Africa. | Explain that first, we will be making a variation of a recipe called Hoppin’ John. Go through the rest of the Introduction Slides. | Hoppin’ John was an invention of African American cooks in South Carolina. | The primary ingredients are black-eyed peas or red peas and rice. It is traditionally eaten by African Americans on New Year’s for good luck and prosperity in the coming year. | We will be making Hoppin’ John burgers today. Our recipe today integrates ingredients that are from African American cooks. | [For older students]--One cool thing about food traditions is that they change over time. So while making burgers out of Hoppin’ John might be a little bit new and different, we are doing something creative with a traditional set of ingredients. |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Remind students of our safety norms. | Pass out ingredients, tools, and task sheets for the Hoppin’ John burgers. Circulate to help students as necessary. | When students are finished, collect burgers and put them on a tray to bake in the oven. | Students will need to wash hands after shaping the burger patties. |  | Closing ritual (15 minutes): | Give students back their food and help them assemble burgers. | Students eat all together and share tasting words and feedback. | Clean up plates. | 

Grade Level Variations:
Lower grades: 
Younger students will need to be given oral instructions in smaller chunks to make it easier to follow and may be unable to use the task cards.
Younger students may need help mashing the rice and peas.

Back pocket activities: 
Students may finish cooking at different times. For students who finish early, give them other tasks to help with, such as cleaning tables, sweeping floors, emptying compost buckets, or collecting tools. You can assign each seat at each table a number corresponding with each job.
This lesson could exist as an introduction to a larger unit on African American contributions to American food culture. Other topics could include: 
the role of food in the civil rights movement
the invention of free breakfast programs by civil rights groups like the Black Panthers
regional food specialities influenced by African American food innovations
and studies of modern-day African American farmers and cooks.

 (burgers only)
 (burgers only)

Black-eyed peas
burger buns
brown rice
sweet potatoes

bell pepper

salt

Pre-cook rice and peas.
Pre-roast sweet potatoes seasoned with salt, pepper, thyme, and cayenne, if using. 
Arrange materials for each table. Each student will also need a cutting board, safety knife, and scissors. Arrange a plate or container with one per student of each of the following:
For the Hoppin’ John burgers:
¼ cup cooked red peas or black-eyed peas
2 TBSP cooked rice (preferably Carolina Gold)
½ scallion
1 strip of bell pepper
1 slice of cooked sweet potato
Plate or container for measured ingredients
Fork or other tool for mashing

Anticipated problems:
Use a sheet of parchment paper under the burgers while baking. Write students’ names or table and seat numbers on the parchment paper for tracking purposes. 
If the patty ‘meat’ isn’t sticking together, a little oil may help. We set up the plates with beans, rice, and a splash of water which also helped.

How Food Moves (Food Miles)



[Table]
Summary: | Students will play a game in which they are assigned miles based on foods they choose.  They will evaluate whether the winner of the game should be the team with the most or least miles based on their discussion of the impact of each kind of food. |  | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will identify parts of the world that their food comes from, understand that out-of-season food travels a great distance to get to them, and weigh the benefits of buying food locally.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and Community Stewardship, Social Justice, Garden skills and related academic content


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by playing a kinesthetic game to understand content.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills |  | Students practice social awareness by learning about and thinking critically about the impacts of how our food travels. | 


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Students can study a vegetable that they are growing in their own school garden and compare the “food miles” of the commercial vegetable vs their garden grown one


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students will have to demonstrate their knowledge of other states (and what’s growing where), as well as what is grown in their own state of NY. Students will be asked to assess how far/close different examples of the food system are to them. Students will be asked to think about how they can practice independence (and interdependence) by growing their own food


[Table]
Tags: | Full-year, vegetables, stewardships, food justice, food systems | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow |  | Opening ritual (10 min) |  | Pose a question to the students about a fruit or vegetable that is out of season and why it’s not in the garden right now. “For example, right now if I go look in the garden right now I won’t find any strawberries. Why not?” Take students' ideas. | “The grocery store does have strawberries though! If it’s too cold to grow strawberries here then how does the grocery store have strawberries?” Take students’ ideas. |  | Engaging activity | Introduce the term “food miles” and hand out food miles maps. | Read through the locations and how many miles away they are on the food miles map together as a class. | Have students flip over the food miles map and read the back. The first column tells us when each food is available in New York, and the second tells us where the food comes from when it’s not available in New York. Read through a few examples of foods from the chart as a class. | Explain the rules of the game: | There will be two teams. One member from each team will come up to draw a card. The card will tell you what food you are eating and what month you are eating it in. Teams will look at the food miles chart to figure out if that food came from New York or somewhere else and how many miles it traveled. | Put labels on the floor to represent the food coming from different places (i.e. a marker right at the front of the room to show New York--0 food miles, and one far away to show that it’s coming from Chile--5,000 food miles. When students pick a card and figure out where the food comes from, they go stand by that label on the floor and pretend to fly or drive the food back to the teacher in New York. | Play several rounds of the game, keeping track of how many food miles each team has, as permits. | When the game is over, tally up the food miles for each team. Ask the students which team won. Is it better to have more food miles or less? Discuss the pros and cons with the students (more food miles means more pollution from transporting the food, less food miles means less variety--we wouldn’t have mangoes or bananas). |  | Closing ritual: | Challenge students to look at the stickers on fruits or vegetables the next time they are home or at the store to figure out where the different foods they eat are coming from. | Finish with a tasting. |

Extension:
Have students write persuasive essays about if it is better to eat foods from far away, local foods, or a mix. 
Have students collect stickers from fruits or vegetables and bring them into class to share out about where their food comes from.

Hummus and Pita


[Table]
Summary: | Students will read a story by a Palestinian author about olive trees in Palestine and how hummus is an important food in Palestinian culture since the main ingredients (chickpeas, olive oil, sesame) are native plants in that region and indigenous Palestinian people have stewarded this land, cared for these native plants and survived on this food for many generations. Students will then work in teams to follow a recipe for hummus. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will understand the concepts of native plants/foods and make connections between native people that steward the land and the recipes that people make with native plants that are culturally significant. Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe. | 


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, cultural diversity.


[Table]

[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Relationship skills in taking turns, working in groups, and displaying compassion for students who may be personally affected by the themes/regions in this lesson, responsible decision-making through using tools safely and considering safety and well-being of self and others, social awareness through learning about and discussing social justice issues and empathizing with others from diverse backgrounds and cultures.


[Table]
