Students can use garlic that is harvested from their garden if that is available (this also can be a way for you to explain cultivation of crops over time - planting garlic bulbs in the fall, harvesting in the spring, this is something that helps us feel connected to our land over time through the seasons).


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson challenges students to apply their knowledge of indigenous people to indigenous people and nations globally, not just in the Americas. This lesson encourages students to think about the autonomy and ownership they have over their garden/land and use that lived experience to empathize with indigenous people who have cultivated land for years and are being displaced from that land.

Think about Native Americans.” | Connect this idea with today’s lesson - “We are learning about indigenous people today. Indigenous people have a very strong connection with the land and a lot of what we know about taking care of the earth and growing plants is because of indigenous people who passed down that knowledge!” | “You may have heard about Palestine in the news lately.” | Give a brief explanation of today - “there is a terrible conflict over sharing land and natural resources, including water, olive trees, chickpeas and lots of other plants that are important to making hummus - what we are making today - and other types of Middle Eastern foods. Both Palestinians and Israelis feel ancestral connections to the same land, and need this land and its natural resources to survive (food, water, shelter). However, Israel is forcefully occupying the land, which means displacing Palestinian people off their land, to a “diaspora,” which means to countries all over the world), even though Palestinians have lived in Palestine for many generations. | Palestinian people getting pushed off of their land is very sad - it means Palestinian people cannot take care of their land, tend to their plants, make food from their crops, etc.” | Emphasize that when people get pushed off their lnd it means they can’t care for the land, plants, and crops. |  | “Today we are going to read a story about someone who grew up in Palestine a long time ago - in 1967. In order to understand what is happening there today, we have to understand what happened in the past.” | “We can’t talk about all the history of the Palestinian people today because we also want to cook, so if you have any questions at the end of our reading, we will give you a paper to write it down and we will make sure to give the answers to your teachers to give to you.” | “Today, we want you to think about why Palestinian people feel connected to Palesitine, and generally why people feel connected to their homeland.” | For 4&5 graders: | Remember when we did our food memory activity? You may have written about an important cultural food to your family. |  | For all: | Food is a very powerful way to connect to where we come from, even if we are far away from that place. |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): |  | First activity (15 minutes reading and discussion) |  | Read | These Olive Trees | by Aya Ghanameh & discuss what the story means to students. | During reading, ask students if they know how long it takes for an olive seed to become a tree with olives?” It takes about 3-10 years! | Ask students if they know how long it takes an olive tree to produce olives or grow? It takes an olive tree 3 years to start producing olives and it takes a tree around 15 years to grow into its full size. Olive groves, areas where farmers cultivate lots of olive trees, take many years of care and tending to make sure they are healthy and produce food that people can eat. | Ask the class: | What are your initial reactions to this story? How did it make you feel? What did it make you think about? | Why has Oraib and her family taken care of this land over so many years? Why is it important for people to take care of the land they are living on? What are some ways you all take care of land - at home, school, in your community? What are some ways the land takes care of you? | 3. Turn and talk: | How does Oraib and her family feel when they have to leave the land? Why? What would be difficult about leaving land that you or your family lived on for many many years? | Do you all think it's important to share land and resources with people that are different from you and why/why not? How could we do a better job of sharing land with our neighbors in our lives, even if they are different from us? | Pass out sticky notes and give students a few minutes to write down any questions about Palestine/Israel/ | These Olive Trees | . | Explain that we only have enough time to read this story and cook a recipe today but we know this is a big and confusing topic and we will do our best to answer any questions later and that they should keep asking questions at school and at home if they come up. |  | Second activity (10 minutes cooking) | Make Hummus | - | Remind students about the importance of trying new foods and not yucking anybody’s yum, different families eating different foods, etc. Ask if anyone eats hummus at home or school or knows what it is (they serve it at lunch sometimes!). Describe hummus and introduce ingredients and directions. Choose group roles (upper grades). | 4&5 graders get | task sheets |  | 2&3 graders do one job per table |  | Students work together to prepare the hummus at their table (Add pinches of salt, garlic, cumin and tahini/lemon juice, mash it) | *K-2 students: Pass out supplies to each table group and introduce each ingredient one at a time, having each student take turns adding each ingredient to the bowl. At the end, everyone takes a turn mashing while singing the “mash it” song | Students slice cucumbers and carrots to dip in the hummus |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Students try the hummus with chopped vegetables and pita bread | Vote on how it tasted (loved/liked/tried it) | Presenters in each group share reflections about how their team worked together, what they did well and what was challenging. | Reflection questions: | Where else can you get hummus or chickpeas to eat? (at lunch!) | What are other types of dips that people have made or tried? | Why are chickpeas and other beans good for our bodies? | What is one thing you learned today that you will share with a younger sibling/student or family member?

Grade Level Variations:

2-3: Do one job per table.

4-5: Get task sheets.

Anticipated problems:
Students might disagree on how much seasonings to put in and end up using too much or too little for other team members’ taste. Remind students to use “pincher fingers” and not pour the whole bowl of spices in. They should taste test as they work to know if they need more seasoning
Students get confused that the vegetables are for dipping and try to put them inside the hummus
This is a complex and emotional topic to discuss. Students may disagree on whose land it is and who is “right/wrong” -  the discussion and reading may take up more time than the cooking. Could be worth it to have a batch of hummus and pita ready to eat in case there is no time to cook
Sesame allergies are common so feel free to leave out tahini / have it as a side dish to add into hummus if there are students with allergies in your class.

Background information for instructor:
-Ask if anyone in the class has heard of Palestine or what has been going on there recently? Explain that we all have different connections to Palestine - some of you may be from there or know someone that is from there, or you may have heard about it from your family/the news. Others of you might not have a connection to this place or know a lot about it. 

Palestine is a country in the Middle East, and Israel is a country that has been occupying Palestine - living on Palestinian land and pushing Palestinian people off - for the past 75 years, since 1948 (Israeli Independence Day/Nakba Day for Palestinians). This has created a serious conflict between the Palestinian and Israeli people for many generations and this conflict has escalated into wars at multiple times in history, including over the last 6-7 months since last October. 

Explain that in Palestine today, there is a terrible conflict over sharing land and natural resources, including water, olive trees, chickpeas and lots of other plants that are important to making hummus - what we are making today - and other types of Middle Eastern foods. Both Palestinians and Israelis feel ancestral connections to the same land, and need this land and its natural resources to survive (food, water, shelter). However, Israel is forcefully occupying the land, which means displacing Palestinian people off their land (to Gaza, the West Bank, other surrounding Arab countries, to a diaspora which means to countries all over the world), even though Palestinians have lived in Palestine for many generations. Palestinian people getting pushed off of their land is very sad - it means Palestinian people cannot take care of their land, tend to their plants, make food from their crops, etc. It is also leading to the death of many people in this region (mostly Palestinian people but also Israeli people and people in surrounding Middle Eastern countries - Lebanon, Egypt, Jordan, Syria, etc.), lots of peoples’ physical and emotional suffering, and the destruction of a lot of land and natural resources - water, olive trees, plants - that all people in the region depend on to make food that is important for their survival and to keep their cultural traditions alive. Emphasize that when countries fight in wars/use bombs, this is devastating to not just people, community and social infrastructure but the natural ecosystem.

Explain that today we are going to read a story about a Palestinian girl named Oraib who helps her family care for olive trees and groves (big areas of land/farms with many olive trees growing) that they have been taking care of for years, but then she and her family have to leave their land/olive trees during a war. Olive trees are very important to Palestinian people - as a plant, a food, a source of business and income, and as a part of their culture. We are making a recipe today, hummus and pita, that is a really important food in Palestine. It's also an important food to many different Middle Eastern countries. Hummus is made from chickpeas, which are a vegetable that are grown all over this region, because they thrive in that climate/environment. People who live in Palestine and in other Middle Eastern countries have taken care of and eaten chickpea plants, similarly to how they have taken care of olive trees, for many years.

[Table]

[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Welcome students back to the garden.  Ask students if they have seen anything exciting growing in the garden yet this spring, or something they are excited to see growing.  Go over expectations for the garden: We are Kind, We are Safe, We are Brave, We are Respectful. Give students a petal point to focus on this period (this lesson works especially well for brave and safe).  (5 minutes) | Today, we are going to learn about insect habitats | . (10 minutes) | Can someone tell me what an insect is?  If needed, help prompt students by asking them some insects they know already and what they have in common. | An insect is a small animal with SIX legs, THREE body parts, and TWO antennae.  Sometimes, they have wings too. | Do we think a worm is an insect?  What about spiders? Ants? Grasshoppers? | What about habitat?  Has anyone heard that word before? | Habitat is the place where a plant, animal, or insect lives.  Where do you think insects live? | Prompt students with different insect suggestions as needed (i.e. where do you think butterflies might live? Ants? Wasps?) | In the garden, we want to create habitat for insects that help our garden grow.  Today, we’re going to learn about some of these insects and the different habitats they live in. |  | NOTE: |  | Remind students that today, we are going to practice all our petal rules, but pay special attention to the brave petal.  Sometimes, insects can make us feel a little nervous!  If you’re feeling nervous today, stop what you are doing, take a deep breath, and hug yourself tight.  That’s how we are going to be brave and safe today around insects. |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Hand out worksheets and nature habitat trays with various plastic insects hidden in them | . (time) | Explain how to complete the worksheet–for younger grades, have students complete a simple bingo where they walk around to different areas of the garden looking for insects and circle when they find them.  For older students, have students complete the habitat worksheet where they write down observations about where each insect lives. | Remind students not to pick up insects–these insects are made of plastic, but picking up real insects isn’t safe.  Explain that this is because some insects can sting, and we are practicing being safe today around insects.  We are insect detectives today–use your eyes! | If students finish early, hand out trays or paper plates for students to make their own insect habitats to attract certain insects. (time) |  | Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes): | What is one thing you learned about insects today?  What is one thing you did to be brave today in class? | (2 minutes) | Clean up, wash hands and give out a tasting. (8 minutes)

Grade Level Variations–
A version of this can be done with 3k-pk, just have fewer habitats (flowers, leaves, and compost).  Hand out magnifying glasses and poking sticks to students to explore.  Ask them to help count legs, observe colors, or identify insects.  This activity can also be paired with the for Pk-1st grade.  
For older students, you can use real specimens or specimens in resin to make it more difficult. insects in resin butterflies in resin 12 insects resin have students identify good vs bad bugs? https://www.amazon.com/Macs-Field-Guide-Garden-Northeast/dp/0898867126



Site Based Variations:
If you have to bring this activity indoors, you can bring in trays or bins of nature stuff from the garden to make pretend habitats in.  Place the habitats around the classroom and hide the insects in different bins.  

Back pocket activity:
If students finish making their habitats, they can place their habitat in the garden somewhere and look for the insects they want to attract.  Alternatively, have students draw their habitat and/or insects on blank paper.  

Materials: 
1-2 sets of Plastic insects
Plastic trays x5 (enough for each ‘habitat’, leaves, flowers, compost, aquatic, rocks)
Worksheets
Dry erase markers for bingo or pencils for worksheet #2
Nature materials for habitat
Optional: popsicle sticks and magnifying glasses
Extra natural materials for students to make their own habitats

Print worksheets and hide plastic insects around the garden.

Students may struggle with anxiety around insects–give lots of reminders about how to be brave around insects, and how to take breaks when you need it!
Make sure you have enough growing in the garden for habitats (harder to do in early spring).

Jam and Jelly: Fruit Preservation


[Table]
Summary: | Students will learn about preservation of food, specifically how berries and fruits can be preserved through the canning process to make jam/jelly. Students will watch a video, read a book, and spend the rest of the class making personal jars of jam/jelly to take home. | Note: This lesson requires a follow-up class for a tasting of the completed jam or you will need to pass out the completed jam to students’ classes to take home and taste at home after class. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | In this lesson, students will be able to identify that canning and jam/jelly making is a food preservation technique that has been an important part of many cultures for years, learn how to follow a jam/jelly recipe and make their own jar of jam/jelly and practice their cooking skills, like using knives and juicing. | 


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, cultural diversity.


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, because it gives students an opportunity to share family/home culture and experiences or memories with food preservation, specifically canning and making jam/jelly. It communicates high expectations because it expects students to read a recipe and task cards and complete tasks during class time. This lesson is able to be adapted to specific cultures - within or beyond NYC - that have specific customs or traditions around making jam/jelly.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | This lesson encourages self-management since it challenges students to take small risks in trying new foods, using new tools and learning a new cooking/food preservation technique. It also encourages relationship skills, especially taking turns, sharing tools, and working in groups. It encourages patience since this recipe is completed over a few days and students have to wait to see and enjoy the end results of the recipe.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson makes connections between fruits, some of which are grown in our garden (strawberries, blackberries) and a cooking strategy to preserve these fruits to last longer. Since many of these students do not have access to fresh fruits and produce outside of the school garden, this also gives them a new way to cook and eat fruits that last over time.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to think critically about creative ways that people from all cultures preserve food, for many different reasons (culinary diversity, lack of access to fresh food, difficult financial/economic circumstances). It encourages them to work independently and in groups to make connections between the stories about jam they are reading, make jam on their own, and make creative labels for their jam jars.


[Table]
Tags: |  | Winter Recipe, Food preservation, Canning, Dehydrated, Fruits, Jam/Jelly | Cooking skills: Shredding/grating, Simmering, Mashing, Stirring, Main ingredients: Strawberries/any other berries work too (fresh or frozen), Apples, Lemon Juice, Honey (other sweeteners work too - agave or maple syrup), Heating element: stove. | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (5 minutes): |  | Hi everyone, welcome back to cooking class. How are you feeling today? Show me with your thumbs. |  | Raise your hand if you like eating jam/jelly. What flavor of jam or jelly do you like? Strawberry jam? Blueberry jam? What’s your favorite fruit? Do you think that would taste good as a jam? How do you eat your jam or jelly? On toast? In a sandwich? | Has anyone ever made jam/jelly before? Do you remember what you did to make it? | Give students a few minutes to share relevant stories about times they have eaten or made jam and follow up about their food memories - where did you eat this? Who were you with? Did they make it or did you buy it? | Share a personal story - ex: I used to make plum jam with my Grandpa in Berkeley, CA because he had a plum tree in his backyard and the tree produced too many plums to eat. He would make jam so that he could preserve all of the plums and eat them throughout the year even when the tree wasn’t producing fruit. |  | Engaging activity (35 minutes): | Making jam/jelly is a type of food preservation, this means a way to save fresh fruit from going bad. Have you ever gotten a fruit like a strawberry from the grocery store and it starts to spoil at home within a few days? Fresh fruits, especially if they are coming from farms far away, can spoil quickly and then we can’t eat them anymore. Food preservation - drying/dehydrating, canning, pickling, fermenting - are all ways for us to preserve food so that we can keep it for a long time. Jars of jam and jelly are made with fresh fruits, but can last a long time on our shelves or in our fridges. | Emphasize that making jam/jelly is really yummy, but also can be a way to save money if people do not have enough money or access to fresh fruits. | Does anyone have any questions about food preservation? | What fruits do we grow in our garden that could be preserved? | Does anyone remember making pickles last year? This is one type of food preservation and today we are going to make jam/jelly. | Read a book and have a discussion (10 minutes). | Read age appropriate books (linked in Grade Level Variations below). | Ask students: What did you think about this book? Why did the characters in the book decide to preserve fruits to make jam/jelly? What did they need to do to preserve jam/jelly? Do you have any other questions or comments about the book? | Watch the video (5 minutes). If time, watch | “From Strawberry to Jam” | by Lisa Owings to go over the step by step process of how to make jam/jelly. | Make jam in small groups (20 minutes). | Review the ingredients, | recipe sheet | and jobs that students will do in small groups to make strawberry jam with | task cards | . | Go over kitchen safety - especially how to hold and use a grater safely and to be careful around the stove/hot plate. | Students will start jam (will need time beyond class to reduce and cook). | Teachers circulate to work with tables to ensure students are working together, discussing, following directions, and equitably splitting up responsibilities, etc. | Decorate canning labels with coloring materials - label what type of jam it is, what ingredients are in it, and encourage students to come up with creative names/brands for their jam. | You also can have one group make jam and one group decorate and have them switch stations. |  | Closing ritual (5 minutes): | Eat a dried fruit tasting - pass it out and say “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.” Explain that dried/dehydrated fruit is another type of food preservation. | You also could do a tasting with a few different types of pre-made jam. | Ask, do you like eating dried fruits? What are your favorite fruits to eat dried? Are there any fruits that taste better fresh? Any fruits that taste better dried? | How is everyone feeling? Did you have fun making jam/jelly today? You all did a great job working together. | What was one new thing you learned today about making jam/jelly? | When your jam is finished, we will bring it to your classroom so you can take it home. What are you going to do with your jam? Will you eat it? Will you give it to someone? If so, who will you give it to?

Grade Level Variations:
3K-PK:
Read Purple Delicious Blackberry Jam by Lisa Westburg Peters. Pre-shred the apples and measure out the berries, lemon juice and honey. Have the students focus on identifying and mixing the ingredients together and singing the Mix It Mix It Mix It song.

K-1:
Read Blueberries for Sal by Robert McCloskey. Have students wear gloves and help with shredding apples a few at a time. One student can hold the grater while the other one grates the apple. You can have students come up one at a time to stir the jam on the stove.

2-3:
Read Jam and Jelly by Holly and Nellie. Students can work together to shred but make sure they are wearing gloves and working slowly and carefully. Focus on measuring ingredients and make sure they understand that they can add in more honey if they want it to be sweeter. Use task cards. Focus on concepts of food preservation and the reasons why people would preserve fresh fruit into jam/jelly. 

4-5:
Read Jam: A True Story by Margaret Mahy. Students can be given more responsibility in shredding, measuring, mixing, and stirring on the stove. Use task cards. Emphasize that food preservation is a cross-cultural cooking technique, that people from different countries and cultures across the world have made jam/jelly for a long time to preserve fresh food. Ask students to think critically about their neighborhood - what fruits grow here? What fruits do we have access to? Where are these fruits coming from? What is the food production and distribution process like and why do fresh fruits go bad by the time they get to the city? Emphasize that food preservation is a way to preserve food but also to save money since fresh fruits are expensive and can go bad quickly.

Tell students we are learning about ladybugs today. | Read a ladybug story book, such as | Am I Really Different? | by Evelien van Dort. | In the story, ask students to empathize with the ladybug who is teased for only having one spot. “How do you think she feels?” | Afterwards, have students name some of the ways that they are different from their friends. Look at the pictures of ladybugs from the book, and count how many dots they have. Students can choose a number of dots to put on their ladybug crowns. Help students draw spots and color in their ladybug crowns. |  | Staple or tape students’ hats together in a circular crown, and help them put them on. Tell them that their job is to each find an aphid that is hiding, and bring it back to the whole group to pretend to “eat.” Show students an example of an aphid picture, and explain that the pictures are hidden within the garden or the room. | Help students to each find a hidden aphid picture, then come back to the whole group to pretend to eat the aphid. |  | Closing ritual (5-10 minutes) | Thank the ladybugs for helping to save our garden. | Finish with a tasting |

[Table]

[Table]

[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Ask students if they know what season it is. Ask them to observe how the garden has changed since summertime. Emphasize changes such as leaves changing color, leaves falling, temperatures cooling, and animals hibernating. | “Our job as gardeners is really important when the seasons change. The garden needs us to help get it ready for the next season. Today we will be helping the garden get ready for winter!” | Explain to students that since the leaves are falling from the trees and plants, we will be doing an important garden job today. We will be collecting fallen leaves to give to the worms in the compost and help to care for our garden. |  | Engaging activity: |  | Help students put on garden gloves, to protect their hands when picking up leaves. Bring students to the place in the garden where they will be picking up leaves, and show them how to place the leaves in the bags or buckets. Students can also help one another by holding the bags open for each other, or pressing the leaves down into the bag or bucket. Make sure students only pick up leaves that have already fallen, not leaves that are still on trees or plants. | If possible, dump the leaves in the compost at the end. Explain to the students that these leaves are going to be food for worms, and the worms are going to turn it into soil. They may not fully understand this concept, but it’s okay for now to just briefly visit the compost. |  | Closing ritual: | Ask students to reflect on the work they did in the garden today. How did it make them feel to feed the worms and clean up the garden? Remind students how they helped take care of the garden today. | Finish with a tasting. If possible, explain that some leaves from the garden can also be food, and taste a leaf.

Site Based Variations, if applicable
At 216, you can set up buckets around an open area and have students collect leaves independently. Make sure you tell the students where the boundaries are so they don’t walk too far. In smaller gardens, students can use the whole space.

Back pocket activities:
Read a fall book, such as Red Leaf, Yellow Leaf by Lois Ehlert; Time to Sleep by Denise Fleming; or When Winter Comes by Nancy Van Laan.
Another option is to mulch garden beds using the dried leaves. Move filled buckets and bags around a garden bed, and show students how to place the dried leaves around existing plants in the bed to protect the soil for winter.

(2-3) | Hand out prints of | Chinese Zodiac Calendar | so they can find what animal corresponds to their birth year! | For people who celebrate the Chinese Lunar New Year they celebrate in many different ways. (15 mins) | They will mix the flour and salt, then add the water, knead the dough (if one table is working to make one batch they can separate the dough into however many people are there and they can knead dough separately) | Once you get to the step to let sit in oil and rise for 1 hour, hand them premade dough to create the noodles. | Show them how to make the noodles | Gather their noodles to boil in one pot or table by table. | While noodles are cooking, have them make the sauce (15mins) | Teacher and helper cook noodles, drain, plate them, hand them out to the students when tables are clean and sauce is ready |  | Closing ritual with SEL component: | Show me with your fingers how much you enjoyed making Hand Pulled Noodles! Measuring Championships(King Arthur material is restricted use)


[Table]
Summary: | Students will practice techniques for measuring dry ingredients using cup measures by playing a competitive teams-based game. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to correctly identify basic dry ingredients and correctly measure ingredients within a certain time frame.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | kitchen skills and related academic content |

[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | (pick all that apply or add your own) | : self-management (safety), collaboration, pride, joy | 


[Table]
Garden Connection: | 


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? Students must independently measure out the different ingredients | 


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : | If cooking | : measuring skills, cup measures, dry ingredients, game | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand | -outs and | include estimated time) | Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson | .(15 minutes) | Welcome students back to class.  Go over the 4 rules for garden class, and point out which petal point they will be trying to get today (or whatever other classroom management technique you have!)  This lesson works especially well for students to practice being kind to one another while playing a competitive game. | Have students sit at tables.  There should be no more than 5 students per table. | Explain that today, we are going to play a game called “Measuring Championships”.  In this game, tables will work together to try and get the best score for their team. | There will be 4 rounds–each round, your team has a chance to earn two points.  One point will be awarded to teams that choose the correct dry ingredient from their bowls.  Show the class an example of a dry ingredient bowl from their tray. | One point will be awarded for teams that correctly measure their ingredients. Everyone at your table will get one measuring cup or spoon.  Show the class the measuring tools, and go over the labels for each briefly. | Write an example on the small whiteboard (1 CUP RICE).  If I am holding the 1 cup measure at my table, that means it is my turn to measure.  What ingredient am I going to choose?  That’s right, rice. | Scoop a cup measure of rice that is underfilled, and ask the class “Is this full?”.  No, it is not full.  I won’t get full points for this! | Show students an example of a cup that is overfilled.  “Is this full?”  If students respond yes, agree but show them how it is a little too full. | Carefully scrape the top off back into the bowl so that it is perfectly flat at the top.  Now that I’m satisfied with my work, I’m going to put this cup on the judges table (basket/bin at each table), and raise my hand to have a ‘judge’ look at the measurement.  If my ingredient and measurement is correct, I will get a perfect score–2 points for my team. |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): | Hand out cup measures to tables first, and have them practice identifying which measurement is which.  If students are having trouble reading fractions, remind them that 1 over 2 is the same as 1/2 , etc etc.  Remind students that TBSP stands for tablespoon, and TSP is teaspoon. | Once you feel confident that the majority of students are ready to begin, hand out ingredient trays so students can start guessing which ingredient is which. | Start the game by writing an easy first measurement on the whiteboard.  Check their work, and correct as necessary. | Tally up points as you go on a smartboard or large piece of paper that everyone can see. | For an extra challenge or a tiebreaker point, set out flour for the students to measure. | To win full points, students should use the | fluff, sprinkle, and scrape | method - depending on their familiarity, you can demonstrate and/or hint at this method first. |  |  | Closing ritual with SEL component (5 minutes): | Congratulate students on how hard they worked to practice measuring, and call out tables that worked especially well together as a team.  Raise your hand if you think you were kind to others in class today? | Hand out snack and prizes for winning teams.  “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!” | 

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
This lesson can be adapted for many ages! Here are some ideas:
3rd grade: students may need a little more help with fractions and measuring successfully, so account for only 3 rounds of the championships!

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
If students are moving quickly through the rounds, add in another 1-2 measuring cups or spoons as an extra challenge.  You can also start adding more complex measurements (i.e. 1 ½ cups cornmeal, etc).  Go into “overtime” at round 5 or 6 if they’re still engaged!

Materials: 
3 measuring cups (1 cup, ½ cup, ¼ cup), enough for one of each for each table
2 measuring spoons (1 tbsp, ½ tsp), enough for 1 of each for each table
4-5 bowls of dry ingredients of different textures (rice, beans, cornmeal, dried fruit, oats)
Rice
Guandules
Blue Cornmeal
Dried Cherries
Lentils
Flour (extra challenge or tiebreaker)
Small whiteboard tablet or printer paper on a clipboard (for writing out the measurements for each round)
Plastic trays (optional, but useful for setup)
Large whiteboard, paper or smartboard with table names listed for keeping score
Sticker prizes or other small incentive for winning teams

Prep work and setup:
Prep ingredient bowls, write scoreboard.  

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Some teams may get frustrated if they fall behind early in the championships.  To motivate teams to participate, explain that in the final round, there will be a chance for teams to make up points.  Final round additions could be: an extra point for tables that were especially kind to each other, extra point for teams that guess the mystery ingredient, double points in the final round.

Mural Painting 101: Beautifying the Garden


[Table]
Summary: | Students will learn about the importance | of using art to take ownership of and take care of public spaces, and begin the process of painting a mural in their garden. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to recognize the garden as a community and public space, and share their ideas and dreams for an artistic vision that represents them & their community.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, cultural diversity | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture, because it invites students to reflect on their heritage and cultural experiences, and represent family, community, and neighborhood symbols and values in a mural. It also promotes student-centered instruction, because it encourages students to generate ideas for the mural and work together to combine ideas and create an image and message that they all relate to and support.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | This lesson encourages self-awareness, because it encourages students to feel pride and ownership over their garden space, as well as social awareness, because it creates physical space in the garden to discuss and represent diverse backgrounds and cultures in the school community.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson utilizes physical garden space (on a wall, shed or other big surface), for the students to create their mural. This draws a connection between a collaborative art project, and the collaborative work that they do to take care of the garden throughout the year.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students and community members to think critically about what values are important to them and their school community/neighborhood, what messages/images/art styles they would like to have on the walls in or outside or their schools. This gives students a chance to share their ideas and art skills individually, work collaboratively, and think critically about social and political causes and messages that they care about and want to share.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | Spring garden job, Spring harvest festival/holiday, Any heritage month, Stewardship, Community | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: (10 min) |  | Welcome students back to garden and kitchen class and review class expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” | “Today we are going to be working on an art project, we’re going to be painting a mural. Raise your hand if you are an artist/like making art. What type of art do you like to make? Raise your hand if you know what a mural is. Does anyone want to share what a mural is? What is your favorite mural you have seen in your neighborhood?” | Give students a chance to share. Explain that a mural is a big painting on a wall, sometimes inside and sometimes outside, that people work on together to represent their culture, history, dreams and ideas. Emphasize that even if you are not an artist, there are many jobs that you can help to make this project happen - thinking of ideas for the mural, thinking of a slogan/message for the mural, helping set up and clean up, etc. The most important part of this project is that everyone works together and it's an art project that is made by a big group of people, like a class or a school, so that you can make your shared public space, like your garden space, extra special and beautiful, and a little piece of all of you will always be here even when new students come to the school. | “What have you worked on in the garden to take care of this space? How did you work together with your classmates and teachers to do this?” | Give students a chance to share about garden jobs, especially ones that needed more than one person (ex: picking up trash together so that the garden is clean and there are places to sit, turning compost to help make soil that will fertilize the plants). | Remind students that this garden is a shared community space that we all take care of so we can enjoy spending time here and eating fruits, vegetables and herbs that grow here. Tell them that adding art to the garden is another way for us to take care of this space, and feel ownership over it. |  | Engaging activity: (30 min) | Read the book, | Hey Wall: A Story of Art and Community |  | by Susan Verde. You can also show this | video | of the book being read aloud. | After the video, ask students why they think that the kids in this story wanted to paint a mural in their neighborhood. Ask them why they worked together on painting together instead of just having one artist paint it by themselves. Ask what they liked about this story. | Emphasize that this project is an opportunity for us to beautify our garden space, since this is a garden space that we all share, take care of and spend time in. “When we work together on projects in public spaces, it helps us all feel more connected to the space and make sure that it is clean, beautiful and taken care of.” Remind students what a “steward” is and that art projects are another way to be stewards of our garden and school. | Show students examples of murals in NYC, and ask them to reflect on what parts of each mural they like and do not like. | What colors/images/symbols/messages do you see? What is the message of this mural and how did they make this clear? (simple short clear messages, dark color on light background to make it pop, using symbols to represent hope/love/peace instead of writing it out like an essay, etc.). You could pass these images around one by one or have them set up around the room like a gallery walk and have students use post-it notes to write their reflections on each image. | You can use pictures of murals in their neighborhood/school, especially youth made murals. | Here are links to | Public Assistants | , | Groundswell | and | Thrive Collective | , NYC organizations that paint murals in the city with youth. You can also show some of these | murals | that are celebrating community gardeners and farmers in the Bronx. If you want to make the mural more relevant to nature, you could use this forest | mural | in the Bronx or this ecosystem restoration | mural | in Manhattan as examples. | Tell students we are going to use the inspiration from all these murals to start thinking about our own mural. Make a collaborative list on a piece of chart paper of: Who is in your community? What cultures and communities are in your community? Are there important symbols/places/landmarks in this community? (kids, adults, teachers, ice cream lady, dog walker, Puerto Rican, Dominican, train station, etc. This can be broad or specific!) | Tell students we are going to do some drawing to brainstorm images for our mural. Remind them how we can use this art project to represent our community and all the people and cultures in it. | Pass out paper and colored pencils/markers and give students 5 min to work independently to pick 3 things from this list, and draw a picture representing these 3 things. It could be a scene in a school, a home, a community, a garden, a park, etc. | When they are done, give them partners and tell them to combine their individual pictures into one big picture with their partner. Hand out butcher/chart paper. You can repeat this again in bigger groups and have students share what they drew. | If you have time, vote on what images/text/symbols/colors you want to include in your mural and start sketching a final sketch. You can have students help write notes to the school/community to tell them about the mural and ask for their help in painting it (pick a date/time). You also could start discussing/voting on where in the garden to put the mural, and discuss if you want it to be connected to garden imagery. |  | Closing ritual with SEL component: (5 min) | Show students a video/picture from this | powerpoint | about social activism and youth activism through art. | Ask students what they think about this project. Why did students do this? Why did they work together? Why do you think they chose art as the way to represent their ideas and voices? | Congratulate students on working together well and listening to each other and combining ideas for this project.

Grade Level Variations

3K-PK: Students this young might have a hard time with fine motor skills, especially painting upright or on a wall. They also are really small so probably can’t reach. Instead of the talking points above,fFocus on discussing what a mural is, looking at examples and sharing things they like and colors they see. They can work on coloring/painting individual drawings or working on a class mural on a piece of parchment paper on the floor. They also can do finger painting or painting with their handprints.
K-1: Focus on what different symbols/images mean and how to communicate a message through a picture (ex: stop sign means stop, we know this because of the word, the shape and the color). Show pictures of murals and ask the students to identify different colors, shapes and messages. They can definitely paint on walls, but have them sit on Xs marked on the floor/tarp a few feet away from each other, and only give them small cups with a tiny bit of paint and a brush in case they spill the paint. Have them fill in bigger portions of the mural that are hard to mess up (like blue sky) and paint little bits on the wall like paint by number so they know where and where not to paint.  
2-3: Focus on the community aspect of mural painting, and all of the different people and roles that go into this project. Discuss how this is a shared responsibility and project and how we are sharing this project with all the different grades and community members. Introduce social justice concepts to them and the idea that art is a form of activism because it can express social, cultural and political ideas. They also can paint on the walls, but have them practice first on butcher paper and also have them sit on Xs and only paint one color at a time and in small groups. Give them tasks that feel doable and not too overwhelming, especially if they are nervous or not confident about their art skills.
4-5: Focus more on the social, cultural and political history and meaning of murals, especially ones in their neighborhood/that represent the communities they come from. They would like to look at murals posted around the room, like in a gallery walk. Have them think critically about what murals say or could say, and what message they would like to represent in their school. They can do more detailed work, but also have them only do one color at a time and have a few students paint at a time while others do a drawing or back pocket activity. You also can have students work in pairs that you choose.
If you are working with older students, you can get into the history of Mexican Muralists. You can also discuss the differences between murals and graffiti. You can research the graffiti artists in the neighborhood you are teaching, show students tags, etc.  

Back pocket activity: 
Paint rocks to decorate the garden (bees, lady bugs, butterflies, or just colors/designs)
Draw/paint signage for the garden
Have students lay down on a piece of chart paper, trace each other, and cut out these shapes. Start mapping out your mural with these cut outs and paper cut outs of other shapes.

Book or Video of reading book aloud
Paper 
Chart/butcher paper
Colored pencils/markers
Pictures of murals (linked above)
Sticky notes
Pens/pencils
Scissors
Tape

You can write questions on posters/butcher paper for the brainstorming section if you want students to brainstorm that way - walk around and answer questions on each paper.

Prep work and setup:
Obtain oyster mushroom spawn (available here). One bag of inoculant was enough for 4 classes in our case, although you could probably stretch it further. 
Pre-soak cardboard in hot water to sanitize it. 
Set out stations with mycelium, cardboard, bags, and rubber bands. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Additional notes from Grow to Learn workshop are available here and here. 
Mushroom bags must be kept moist once they are opened. Spray them periodically. 
Mushrooms can take a while to fruit. Be prepared for it to take 2-3 months.
Mushroom bags should be kept in a dark place to help start inoculation. As the mushroom bags get inoculated, you will see threads of mycelium eating away the cardboard. The mushrooms are ready to fruit when the cardboard is fully inoculated and covered in white mycelium. 
To encourage fruiting, the mushrooms need oxygen. Open the top of the bag and cut holes in the side. This will mean you will need to start watering the mushroom bag because they can dry out.
Harvest mushrooms before the caps start to turn up; after that they will have produced spores, so they will shrivel up and die.

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
If time allows, you could also allow students to harvest some dye materials from the garden. 
This could also be altered into a science experiment, where students could predict the color of dye that each item will produce. Many of these dyes also react to pH, so you could do a controlled experiment in which you add a pH modifier (like lemon zest as an acid or baking soda as an alkaline) and compare that to a dye without pH modifier. 
Design Your Own Clothes Coloring Sheet

Materials: 
Dye visuals
Cotton fabric
String
Scissors
Dye materials (specific suggestions below)
Cups or ramekins for dye materials
Pencils
Cardstock
Stainless steel pot, 
Induction burner, tongs
Alum (optional)

Prep work and setup:
Cut up cotton fabric into squares for students. Cotton fabric should also be washed with a mild soap beforehand to remove any chemicals, like Dawn, Dove, or Dr. Bronner’s.
To make the dye last longer, you can prepare the fabric with a mordant. Alum is a safe mordant for use with kids. To do this, soak the fabric beforehand, then add to a hot water bath (not boiling) with the alum stirred in for about an hour. The amount of alum used should be 10% the weight of the fabric. This step is completely optional; students may not care as much if the fabric fades over time.
Set out trays of dye materials into cups or ramekins with labels.
Cut small pieces of cardstock for labels. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Additional, detailed information is available here and here. 
City Growers Natural Dye Curriculum
Students may need help tying their bundles, but otherwise should be self-sufficient with the activity. 
Some easy, inexpensive, and kid-friendly dye materials are listed in this table. 
After students create their bundles, they should be dipped in water and soaked before going into the hot water bath to dye. This will allow the dye to absorb.
The bundles should stay in the water for at least an hour. The temperature should be between 140-180 F; any higher and the dyes will oxidize and change color. 
After the bundles are done, they should be rinsed of all plant material, rinsed in cold water, and then washed with a mild soap such as Dawn, Dove, or Dr. Bronner’s. They are then ready to be used.

[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students practice self-management by practicing safety in the garden. Students will also explore the garden independently while identifying plants in different stages of their life cycle.

Tags: | seed-to-table, plant life cycle, scavenger hunt | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (15 minutes): |  | Welcome students back to the garden. “This year, we are going to focus on becoming true garden experts. We are going to get really good at knowing how plants grow and how we, as gardeners, can help take care of them. Today we are going to focus on learning about an entire plant’s life cycle.” |  | Ask if students know what the word “life cycle” means. “A cycle is made up of events which go in a circle. A cycle keeps going over and over again.” Point out that “cycle” looks like the word “circle.” | Using student answers, draw up a plant life cycle on the board from seed, to baby plant, to flower, to fruit, to seeds again. “What happens at the end? Does the cycle end? No, it repeats all over again!” | “What happens to the old plant when it dies and decomposes? What does it turn into? Where in the garden do we put it? In the compost!” | Explain that today, we are going to be exploring the garden to see the plant life cycle in action. Have students remind you of garden safety rules and expectations, emphasizing the importance of showing respect for plants, animals, and each other. Tell students you will be looking to see who has been an especially good leader today in being respectful in the garden. |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | Pass out clipboards with scavenger hunt worksheets. Explain that there will be ten signs in the garden with a star on them. Each sign will be next to a plant in one stage of the life cycle. “Your job is to figure out which part of the life cycle it is in, and then draw a picture of it in the correct box.” Go over the worksheet and have students identify the life cycle step for each box, using the visuals if students are still developing reading skills. | You can encourage students to label their drawings with descriptive text if that’s appropriate for your students. | Have students go out into the garden to find the signs. Circulate and assist if needed. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Call students back to the whole group. Have students share their drawings with a partner. | Share a tasting. Have students identify what part of the life cycle the food comes from. | Have students give shout-outs to each other for being respectful in the garden of plants, animals, and each other.

Grade Level Variations:
You can challenge students with stronger writing skills to write descriptive words for the plants they see at each life cycle station. 

Back pocket activity:
If time allows, use a second copy of the worksheet to allow students to draw their own complete version of the plant life cycle. Provide colored pencils or crayons so they can add detail to their drawing. 

Materials: 
White board and dry erase markers
Worksheets and star signs
Pencils
Clipboards
Colored pencils or crayons (optional)
Tasting

Set up star signs around the garden. Choose plants where the life cycle is very apparent. For a seed station, you may want to have seeds set out in a cup or container. 
Optionally, you can set colored pencils or crayons in small buckets or cups at each station so students can add color to their drawings. 
Put worksheets on clipboards.

[Table]
Garden Connection: | Students will study plants that are present in their school garden and surrounding neighborhood

They will be assigned tasks on a worksheet, to complete independently, and they will demonstrate their understanding of each plant and its parts


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: | late summer, fall | For all lessons | : monthly theme, holiday or heritage month (if applicable), region of the world (if applicable) | If cooking | : Cooking skill(s), Main ingredient(s). Heating element: none, stove or oven. | If gardening | : garden theme ( | ie pollinators, soil, seeds, compost, animals, stewardship, vegetables, water, etc) | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow |  | Opening | (5 minutes) | Today you are going to be detectives. You are going to use your senses to investigate different plant parts and try to guess which part is which. | You will get to use three of your senses—sight, smell and touch. AND, because you are detectives, you will get to use some special detective equipment—these hand lenses. | Hold up a plant with roots visible. | Before we begin, let’s remind ourselves about plants.  All plants have several different parts, and each part does something different for the plant. (Make a comparison to the human body and give a few examples of what different parts of our body allow us to do.) | Show students an example or an image of a plant with all of its parts. Ask students what they think each part is for. See what answers you get. See if students have any thoughts on what each part does for the plant. | When you are examining your plant parts, think about what each part does for the plant. Maybe that will help you identify the parts by how they look. | Remember that detectives don’t always figure things out quickly, but good detectives keep trying, even when it’s difficult. |  | Engaging Activity One | (20 minutes) | While the students are still in the circle, hand out worksheets and explain: You will be going from station to station to do your detective work. Each station has one plant part. When you get to the station, some of you can start by looking at the plant with just your eye—and draw what you see. Model this to students. | Some of you can start by looking at the plant with the hand lens—and draw what you see. | When you have examined the plant part with your eyes and with the hand lens, guess which part of the plant you were looking at. | When I say, “Switch!” you will go to the next station on your sheet. | If you have a circle on the top of your sheet, start at the circle station. If you have a square, start at the square station. | Circulate and help students get to the right stations. They might need guidance dividing evenly between the two circle stations, etc., getting started, sharing the hand lenses, etc. |  | Engaging Activity Two | (15 minutes) | Lead students in either of the options below. |  | Option A | Have students turn their worksheets over on their clipboards. Send them out in the garden to examine and draw different plant parts they find there: flowers, leaves, stems, etc. | If possible, you can start by taking them somewhere they can each pull out one plant by the roots and letting them draw the roots. |  | Option B | If | indoors | , tell the students: Now we are going to pass out weeds. You need to cut off the different parts of these weeds and put them in piles: all of the leaves together, all of the stems, etc. You should examine these plants with your neighbors and discuss what parts you think go where. |  | Closing Circle | (10 minutes) | Bring the students together for a closing circle, with their work. | Share what they found. Look at the part of paper that has a circle on it. What plant part do you think you drew there? Why do you think so? | Repeat for each of the plant parts. | I want to congratulate you for being good detectives and trying to figure out all the plant parts, even if you weren’t always correct! | Actually, all of these plant parts have something in common – does anyone know what? (we eat all of them, we can grow all of them in our garden?) | Eating plants is an important part of being healthy—see how many plant parts you can eat this month. | Share a plant part tasting.

If you see any plants as you’re working, try to notice what parts are visible. What parts are not visible or not there? How do these plant parts look similar to the ones you saw yesterday? How do they look different?”)

Sound Ball: Make a circle. One student makes an exaggerated sound and body movement and “passes it” to the next person. They copy the sound and add a new one to pass on. This can go clockwise or across the circle.

Tell them they can also use the potato as a paint brush if they don’t want to make prints/if they are having trouble with motor skills. | “We are all sharing these shapes, so let’s be kind to each other and let each other use all of the different shapes. Let’s also try to be respectful of our classroom and keep the paint on the table - be careful not to get the paint on yourself, your clothes or your friends.” | Optional: Give students a prompt for their potato prints especially if they don’t know what to do (paint a picture of the garden outside, paint a picture of your favorite animal, paint a picture of you eating french fries, etc.) | Pass out paint, paper, water bowls, and potato cut outs. Have the students write names on their papers and make potato prints. | Have teachers/paras help students clean up the table, wash hands, and put their art somewhere safe in the classroom to dry. | While students are painting, cook the french fries in the oven/stove/air fryer and prep plates for them to do a tasting. |  | Closing ritual: (10 min) | “You all did such a great job today harvesting potatoes and making potato art! I loved the way that you took turns harvesting and washing your potatoes outside and that you shared the potato prints during the art project. You all learned so much today about where potatoes come from and different ways to use them. Raise your hand if you want to do these things again at home. Can anyone think of another food you could use for an art project at home?” | Have students share their favorite part of the lesson and/or something new they learned today. | End class with a french fry tasting. Remind students to be careful since they are hot. | Pass out fries and ketchup. | While students are eating, ask them if they want to share any memories of times they had french fries/ where to get the best french fries.

Grade Level Variations–
3K-1: Prepare the french fries for the students while they are making art projects. If using the stamps is challenging for the youngest students, allow them to fingerpaint! 
2nd-5th: Have students help with chopping up potatoes and tossing them with oil, salt and pepper, and with plating and serving fries. You can also have students help with watering/another garden task while other students are harvesting and then have them switch.

Site Based Variations
Lessons can be all inside/outside- you can either do the art project outside in the garden/at tables outside or harvest potatoes in bins of soil in the classroom.

Back pocket activity
Plant Life Cycle Dance to review how plants grow.
Pass out potatoes and have students observe them, touch them and find anything unique about their potato. Tell students to give them names and have them introduce their potato pets to the class. You could have students do this with the potatoes they harvested.
Play hot potato with a potato they harvested.

Materials: 
Shovels/trowels
Garden gloves
Buckets of water
Potatoes
Cookie cutters
Knife
Plates
Paint (washable)
Construction paper or cardstock (also can use cardboard)
Bowls
Water
Paper towels
Cleaning wipes
Oil
Salt
Pepper
Ketchup

Prep work and setup:
Set up buckets of water near the garden beds where students will be harvesting potatoes.
Optional: set up garden gloves and trowels/shovels for students to use to harvest.
Pre-cut potato prints with cookie cutters/a knife and put them onto plates.
Put different colors of paint onto plates.
Fill bowls with water.
Pre-cut potatoes for french fries and toss them in oil and salt and pepper (3K-1)
Prep plates with ketchup.
Prep plates for the tasting.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
You can cut out prints with cookie cutters like this:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3zTr7Y9tDeg 
You can also carve shapes into potatoes like this: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CCwe1HHCISQ 
How to make potato butterfly stamps:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SE6piKYajUQ 
Cleaning up from this activity, especially washing hands, takes longer than anticipated, so leave extra time for this. 
If you are co-teaching, you can also split up the group and harvest with one group, do the art project with the other group, and then switch.

[Table]

[Table]
Core Competencies: | Community stewardship | Social justice | 


[Table]

[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Relationship skills (working well in groups) and social awareness (learning about, discussing and taking action about social justice issues in a broader societal and local sense)


[Table]

Also encourages building stronger student relationships and connections with school workers - where does our food come from? How is what we grow in the garden connected to what we do or do not eat in the cafeteria? How could cafeteria workers be invited to be a part of what we are growing and learning about in the garden/in ESY programming?


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson encourages students to think critically about who does labor, especially labor that they benefit from daily / labor that is essential to the functioning of the school community, and be creative about ways to show gratitude for these workers in a direct and practical way. Students are encouraged to use their imagination and creatively to come up with creative slogans on their signs and creative designs on the aprons.


[Table]
Tags: | S | chool Lunch Hero Day, cafeteria, community, activism | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (15 minutes): | Welcome the students and ask them to give you a thumbs up/down to show how they are feeling. (5 minutes) | Today we’re going to learn about a very special group of people in this school. I like to call them school lunch heroes because they are the workers in the cafeteria who make lunch for all <insert school enrollment> students each and every day! Isn’t that amazing? | Does anyone have family members that work in food service? This could be in a restaurant, cafe, grocery store, or school cafeteria? Give students time to share any relevant stories and remind them – we don’t always know the people who work in food service very personally, but they are all people that are a part of our community (parents, aunts, uncles, etc.) who work very hard. | Does anyone know the names of the people who work in our cafeteria? Why is it important to learn their names? What are ways we can acknowledge them as important people and acknowledge the work they do as important? | Show | a video | that gives students an overview of what a day in the life of a school food worker is like, then check for understanding by having students answer reflection questions. | If you search “Day in the Life of a cafeteria/school food worker, there are many similar videos in different cities/states if you would like to show an alternative or additional video. |  | (10 minutes) | What did you think about this video? What do our school food workers do every day? Did you know all of this before? Did you learn anything new? Do you have any questions still about what they do/anything you would like to ask them directly? | Write down any questions students have for their workers. | Why do you think it’s important that every student gets breakfast/lunch? | How might you feel if you didn’t eat meals at school? | Would you have the energy to do all the things you love to do? | Would you be able to focus if you felt hungry? | What is your favorite meal at school? Have you told a worker this is your favorite meal/thanked them? Why is this an important thing to do? |  | Engaging activity (25 minutes): | The job that our school lunch heroes do is so important that there’s a day celebrated all across America in honor of workers just like the ones in our school cafeteria. It’s called School Lunch Hero Day and it’s coming up in May! | To show our appreciation for our cafeteria workers and celebrate School Lunch Hero Day, we’re going to make signs to hang up in the cafeteria and decorate aprons to give to them. | Walk through the two activities - sign making and apron decoration. Show examples of a sign you have made and a decoration you have added to an apron (see Materials below). | Depending on your class size and space, you could have an area to pull students to decorate the aprons while everyone works on their signs, or you could have everyone work on their signs then transition everyone to work on aprons (1 apron per table). | Note: if doing this with multiple classes/grades, ensure students leave space on the aprons for all students to add their own decoration. | For the signs, you might write prompts on the board that they can use (see Materials). | If students need more time beyond the class period to finish their signs, ask the classroom teacher if they can take them back to their room to do so then put them in your mailbox when complete. |  | Closing ritual (5 minutes): | Bring everyone back to their seats if you have separated them into groups. |  | Raise your hand and tell me 1 thing you learned about school lunch heroes. | Show me thumbs up/thumbs down if you think our cafeteria workers will like your signs. | What is 1 thing you wrote on your sign? | Does anyone have any other ideas of ways we could show workers our gratitude, besides these aprons and signs? What are some smaller, everyday ways to show our gratitude (learning their names, saying thank you, being patient/following the rules in the cafeteria - waiting in line, cleaning up after ourselves, etc.)? | If there’s time, you could hand out a tasting of a fruit they serve on the tray line, like apples or clementines. | You could discuss what types of foods we grow in our garden that we eat in school lunches (fruits, vegetables, herbs). Discuss if we grow enough food for everyone to eat for school breakfast or lunch, why/why not?

Back pocket activity:
If students finish their signs early, have print-outs of relevant coloring sheets and/or word games they can do (linked in Materials below)
Have students make thank you cards that are personalized for cafeteria workers - what is a favorite food / favorite memory in the cafeteria they could share with the workers in their school community? They also could write thank you notes to local deli people/grocery store workers/other food service workers and you could encourage them to give these workers cards after school/on the weekend.
Make a poster compiling memories/notes of why students are grateful (to hang in the cafeteria)

Sensory Scavenger Hunt and 
Orientation


[Table]
Summary: |  | Students | are welcomed to the garden and taught garden expectations. Students will |  | explore the garden on their own. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students learn garden rules and reinforce their understanding of the five senses through guided exploration of the garden. | 


[Table]
Core Competencies: | garden skills and related academic content | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness: | This lesson |  | incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by giving students the chance to explore the garden with their five senses.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | Students practice self-management by practicing safety in the garden and by gaining comfort in a new, unfamiliar environment.


[Table]
Garden Connection: How does this lesson make use of the garden? | This lesson happens in the garden space and orients students to the different ways that they can experience the garden, using all 5 senses.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | This lesson promotes independence by helping students feel comfortable in the garden and fostering feelings of responsibility and freedom in the space.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : welcome back to school | If gardening | : | Stewardship, 5 senses, exploration



[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow (45 minutes): |  | Opening ritual (15 minutes): | Welcome students. Ask students if they can name the five senses. | Go over each of the five senses doing gestures to act out each one (shaking out your hands for touch, tapping your nose for smell etc.). | Ask students to make predictions for each of their senses about what they will see out in the garden (what things can we touch or feel in the garden, what things will we hear in the garden). | Talk about how the garden is like the classroom even though it doesn’t look like the classroom! They will still walk instead of running and use one voice at a time. | In addition, the garden is full of special living things that need special care. Explain how students should walk on paths and not on beds, how they should not pick anything unless their teacher tells them to, and they should be kind and gentle to bugs, including bees. Talk about how to freeze when a bee is nearby. |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | For 3K and PK students, go on a guided walk around the garden. Practice using all the senses, touching plants gently, smelling flowers, looking at bugs, listening to the sounds of the garden. This will help students see the whole garden and orient them to the space. | For K, 1, and 2 students, hand out the scavenger hunt papers. Read over the 4 boxes together as a class. Tell the students they won’t be tasting during the hunt but we will taste all together at the end. Show examples of what to draw and write in each section of the scavenger hunt. | Before sending students to complete their sensory hunt, review the rules for the garden (how to be safe for ourselves, the plants, and the animals). | Send students out to the garden to complete their sensory hunt. Circulate to point things out in the garden to students, and help remind them of the garden rules. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Bring everyone back to their seats. Give students a few more minutes to finish their drawings and help them label their drawings if possible. | Have students share about what they saw in the garden. | Finish with a tasting.

Grade Level Variations
3K-PK: Do not use the worksheet. Just explore the garden as a class, staying together, and follow students' impulses and interests. The goal is to orient students to the garden and help them feel comfortable.
K-2: Use the worksheet and allow students to explore the garden independently and draw and write about what they see, hear, smell, and touch.

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Have students circle pictures of different things they saw in the garen today. Alternatively, print out large version of each picture and go through them together, asking students if they interacted with any of these things today.

Pencils
Sensory Scavenger Hunt papers
Tasting

Print the sensory scavenger hunt papers and put them on clipboards.
