Sun Printing


[Table]
Summary: | Students will become familiar with what flowers, leaves and plant parts are in the garden, and use them to make cyanotypes or sun prints. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to walk carefully and safely around the garden, be respectful of living plants growing in the garden, and arrange their collected plant parts in an artistic and creative way. Students will be able to use natural sunlight to capture images, and understand this as an alternative to digital photography.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content.


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson communicates high expectations of students, by inviting students to walk freely around and collect natural elements safely and carefully, encouraging them to be stewards of the garden and treat the space gently and respectfully. This lesson also promotes student-centered instruction, because students are given the autonomy to pick out what objects they want to include in the project, arrange them creatively, and create diverse prints.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students will practice self-awareness, in the process of building unique projects that reflect their own creative art styles and feeling pride and confidence in their artistic abilities. The students will get to be playful and joyful in the garden and learn that they can make beautiful artistic creations without traditional art supplies.


[Table]
Garden Connection: | This lesson uses plant parts, flowers, leaves, sticks, etc., ideally things that have already fallen into the beds or on the ground, to create an art project. It also incorporates art in outdoor education, showing students that they can make art in the garden or in any outdoor space, using the sun and other natural elements.


[Table]
Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : Garden jobs, food preservation, Spring/summer/sunny days | If gardening | : plants, roots, leaves, stems, flowers, petals, sticks, seeds, sun | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand | -outs and | include estimated time) | Opening ritual: (10 min) |  | Welcome students back to garden and kitchen class and review class expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” | “Today we are going to be doing a really fun art project, called sun printing or cyanotyping. Can everyone repeat the word, cyanotype? This is an art project you can do in any outdoor space, like a park, the beach, or your backyard. The most important thing you need is a sunny day. Can someone share your favorite outdoor place, maybe your favorite park? What does nature look like in that place?” | Give students a chance to share. Provide an example, ex: “My favorite outdoor place is the beach. There’s ocean waves, sand, rocks, sticks, shells, grass, and bushes.” |  | “Can someone tell me how we can be respectful to living plants and animals in our garden?” | Give students a chance to share. | Remind students that plants and flowers are growing in the garden, and that we can’t pull off leaves, flowers, or vegetables, or we will hurt them and they won’t be in the garden for us and other students to enjoy anymore. Tell them that today we will be exploring the garden and collecting things, but we want to all be very gentle with the plants, because this is our garden and we are gardeners, and gardeners take care of their gardens with love and care. |  | Engaging activity: (25 min) | “Today we are going to walk around the garden for 10 minutes, collect some leaves, sticks, and other plant parts that we find, and then we will use them to make a print, which is like a picture. The sun will help create the image, instead of using a camera or phone to take a picture, or drawing the picture by hand. This is an art style that people have been doing for over 200 years! People all over the world, who live in different natural environments, use this art technique, and you can use whatever you have accessible to you. For example, if you are in the park and you only see grass and sticks, you can use that to make your sun print.” | Show students an example of a cyanotype print (maybe one with cyanotype paper and one with construction paper or with whatever materials you are using). | “You can walk around by yourself or with a friend, and collect 5-10 things. Look for things with cool or interesting shapes or textures. If you collect small things, you can include more of them in the picture. If anyone needs help picking things out, or you are not sure if it is something that is alive and growing or not, ask me or another teacher and we can help you.” | Give students 10 minutes to walk around the garden and then have them come back to a central space and spread out and find their own space. | “I am going to pass out paper, and you can arrange your items in a cool design on the paper. Make sure each plant part has its own space on the paper - try not to pile things on top of each other. When you are done arranging your print, take a step back and don’t touch it. It needs a few minutes to sit in the sun, and the sun will print the image of your objects onto the paper. Does anyone have questions before we start?” | Pass out paper and help students arrange their prints. Help students tape down the objects with clear tape if they are not heavy objects and might fly away (leaves, stems, flowers, etc.) | Gather with students somewhere away from the prints so no one touches them. |  | Closing ritual: (5 min) | “You all were so respectful in the garden today, of each other and of the living plants and animals that are in our garden. Did anyone see any animals in the garden beds? How were you respectful of the animals? How were you respectful of the plants?” | Give students a chance to share. | “I saw a lot of students helping each other out today which is so kind. Can someone give a special shout out to a friend that helped you out, either in finding your plant parts or making your print?” | Give students a chance to share. | Tell students to walk carefully over to their prints and take everything off of the picture. Have them share their prints if there’s time.

Grade Level Variations
Older students can work together to make a 3-5 panel cyanotype, that you can string together and hang up in the classroom. You can encourage them to make a collaborative design that spans across multiple papers.
They can also learn more about the history of cyanotyping: https://mfastpete.org/art-break-with-the-mfa-sun-prints/  

Site Based Variations
If you have a shady area in the garden, you can arrange the cyanotypes there and then move them into the sun. If you are using cyanotype paper, the sun starts printing immediately when it is exposed to sunlight, so you have to make the design quickly. If you do it in a shady place, you have more time for students to arrange their designs and still come out nicely.

Back pocket activity:
Write observations about the plants they collected
Leaf tracing/rubbing
Split into groups and make fairy houses out of the natural materials they collected. Have them name the house and share back
Have students put all the compostable materials into the compost and turn/sift the compost

Materials: 
Cyanotype paper (hot press)
Construction paper (dark colors work best)
Plant parts
Clear tape
Cardboard / clipboards
Prep work and setup:
For younger students, you can collect materials / make them accessible or obvious so it’s easier for them to find.
You can tape cyanotype paper/construction paper to pieces of cardboard or clipboards before class (ideally inside or somewhere shady) so the papers don’t fly away, especially if it’s a windy day.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Encourage students to place down their object and then not move it around or touch it too much, so that their print comes out clearly. You can also split up the group and print with half and have the other do leaf/rubbings or another back pocket activity so you can give the students printing more attention. 
Construction paper needs to sit out in the sun for longer periods of time than cyanotype paper. If you use construction paper, maybe have the students collect their prints next time they come to the class.
“Sonoran Desert Sun Prints” by Brit Palomarez in The Sonoran Desert School Gardener’s Almanac could be a way to tie in different geographic landscapes and how that affects culture and art.

The Apple Story


[Table]
Summary: | Students read a story about apples and then illustrate the different steps of the apple’s journey to our table. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to explain some of the steps that food takes to get to us. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness: | incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | social awareness


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Ask students to show you with their thumbs how they are feeling. | Begin class with a tasting, either apple chips or apple slices. | Ask students to share what they have noticed about the garden and the outdoors. Ask: “Are plants growing right now? Why not?” | Show students an apple or draw an apple on the board. Ask students if they have seen apples at the store. Ask: “If I can get an apple from the store, where could that apple be coming from?” Students may answer that the apple was picked somewhere else, or that it grew when it was warm and has been saved since then. | Explain that we are going to read a story today about where apples come from during the winter. |  | Engaging activity: | Read the apple story. Have students act along with the story: pretend to plant seeds, pretend to pick apples, etc. | Tell students that we are going to make our own apple story book. Distribute to each student one coloring page of the apple story. Students can work on coloring and labeling their picture. At the end, you can staple the drawings together in order as a book for the class. |  | Closing ritual: | End with reading the completed story together once more, with students’ illustrations. Ask students to tell you what they learned from the story about some of the steps that an apple has to take to get to us from the farm.

Materials: 
Tasting, either apple chips or apple slices
Apple story coloring sheets
Stapler or paper clips
Crayons or colored pencils

Prep work and setup:
Prepare apple chips, if desired, using the following recipe.
Prepare sufficient copies of your coloring sheets. Each class will end up assembling 2-3 copies of the book, depending on the number of students in your class.

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Students can write books narrating the events it takes for an apple to go from seed to table.
After discussing the path from seed to table of another food (peanut butter, cheese, a carrot), students can write and illustrate this food’s story.

Back pocket activities:
Have extra copies of the coloring sheet available if some students finish quickly and need a second coloring page.

The Honeybee Man



[Table]
Summary: | Students will read a story, then do an art activity to learn about how bees help fruits to grow. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will be able to describe the relationships between honeybees, people, and flowering plants.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by using the story of an urban beekeeper to illustrate environmental concepts, providing a relatable narrative to New York City students.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills | : | Students practice relationship skills by learning to empathize with and understand the role of a common insect that may commonly provoke fear or anxiety.


[Table]
Tags: | pollinators, honeybees, bees, arts and crafts, urban gardening, New York City | 


[Table]
Garden Connection: | Students can make a direct connection to the bees and other pollinators in the school garden.


[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students choose their coloring sheet and take ownership over their work. Students reconsider the common fear of bees by learning about their important role as pollinators.


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual (15 minutes): |  | “All year, we’ve been learning about how important relationships are in our school garden. Today we are going to learn about the relationship between people, plants, and a garden insect: bees.” | Have students turn and talk to a neighbor about how they feel about bees: scared, curious, nervous, happy? Empathize with students’ feelings, then explain that we are going to read a story that might change our ideas about bees. | Read | The Honeybee Man | by Lela Nargi and Kyrsten Brooker. Talk about the relationships shown in the book. | Here | is a pre-recorded read along. | “How did the bees make the honey? Did they do it alone, or did they need to find something to make the honey?” | “How did Fred the beekeeper help the bees? How did the bees help him?” | “What is Fred doing with all the extra honey? Is he keeping it all for himself?” |  | Engaging activity (20 minutes): | “In the book, we saw that the honey bees made honey that Fred could share with his neighbors. In turn, Fred gave the bees a home and took care of them and made sure they were safe. We are going to do a little art project to learn how bees help the plants, too.” | “Do you remember in the book when it said that the bees went to visit flowers on pea plants and blueberry bushes? When the bees drink from those flowers, it helps the flowers change into something else: a fruit!” Show one coloring page visual, with the flower on one side and the fruit on the other. “Without bees, plants wouldn’t be able to make fruits.” | Have students pretend to be bees: buzzing, pretending to have wings, etc. Have students each “visit” a flower to pollinate it and make it into fruit. | You can hang the coloring sheets, flower side out, around the classroom and have students go pick one in small groups. | Or, students can come up in small groups to pick a flower from you as the teacher. | Have students look on both sides of the coloring sheet. “What fruit does your flower turn into?” | Have students color both sides of their coloring page. Older students can also label their drawings. |  | Closing ritual (10 minutes): | Have students turn and talk to a neighbor again: “What is something new that you learned about bees today? Did your feelings about bees change today? How did they change?” | End with a honey tasting. | 

Back pocket activity:
Roll out 1-3 pieces of mural paper, about 5 feet long each, depending on the size of your class. Tell students that we will be making a piece of artwork all together of the story that we read. Ask students to identify important parts of the story that they saw: the beekeeper, the neighbors, the bee, the beehives, the plants, the buildings, etc. Have students work together to draw a mural showing all these parts working together in the story. 
If students need more structure, they can color these images from the story.

Materials: 
The Honeybee Man by Lela Nargi and Kyrsten Brooker
Coloring pages
Colored pencils, crayons, or other coloring materials
Honey for tasting, either in cups or using small disposable wooden spoons

Prep work and setup:
Make photocopies of coloring pages. Print double-sided, short-edge binding, so that the correct fruit and flower will be on both sides of the half-sheet of paper.
Set up honey tasting. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: None.

Back pocket activity: Garden Haiku
Have students write a short haiku poem about their favorite part of the garden
Remind students about the rules of a haiku: Haikus consist of just three lines (tercet); the first and third lines have five syllables, whereas the second has seven. Haikus don’t have to rhyme! 
Example of a garden haiku: 

I see the first leaf (5 syllables) 
On my dear favorite tree (7 syllables) 
Saying hello friend (5 syllables)

Some classes will be too squirmy to sit for a book.  If this happens, you can sing a song instead, to the tune of : 
Sleeping little peapods sleep all winter long
Should i come and wake them with a little song
They’re so still
Are they ill?
Wake up little peapods!  Climb little peapods climb climb climb
Climb little peapods climb climb climb
Climb little peapods climb climb climb
Climb all springtime long
You can also sing this for other cover crops–clover, buckwheat, radishes, etc. and instead of “climb”, ask students to “root”, “sway” or “grow”.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: |  | For all lessons | : Central America, South America, Native American, Caribbean | If cooking | : Preservation, fermentation, herbs, flowers, ginger. Heating element: stove (optional) | If gardening | : decomposer, harvest, preservation |

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time) | Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson | . (5 min) | Welcome back! Does anyone want to share their favorite drinks with the class? | Today we’re going to be talking about soda! I’m sure you’ve heard before that soda is unhealthy for you - this is true because soda contains much more sugar than your body can process normally and it doesn’t have any positive nutrients to help your body. The strain of too much sugar on your organs can lead to many health problems including diabetes, heart disease, dental issues, and more. | Today we’re going to learn how to make wild soda, which can actually be helpful to our bodies! The soda you get at the store is made with carbonated water and flavored sugar syrup. We’re going to use honey and herbs in water to get the flavor and the health benefits directly from the plants. |  | Engaging activity: | Let’s learn more about how this works (show | visuals | ) (15 min) | We’re going to start by talking about the microbiome. A microbiome is a group of microorganisms like bacteria, fungi, and viruses that live in a certain environment. We each have our own unique microbiome based on our DNA, where we live, and what we eat. | For this class, we’ll focus on the gut microbiome in our stomach and intestines. Trillions of organisms in our gut help digest food and absorb nutrients we need. They also help our immune system fight diseases and infections and may even affect our mental health. Other animals and plants also have microbiomes. | Eating a good mix of prebiotic and probiotic foods sustains the good bacteria in our microbiome and keeps us healthy. A diverse, plant-based diet is ideal! | The wild soda we’re making is a probiotic because it contains good bacteria from the plants. It also has wild yeast in its microbiome. Yeast is a cooking ingredient we use to make bread rise. When yeast eats sugar and breaks it down, it makes carbon dioxide gas bubbles - this is called fermentation. The bubbles make bread light and airy instead of dense and heavy. For example, yeast makes pizza dough fluffy and bubbly instead of a flat cracker. Making the bubbles in water causes the water to fizz. | To make soda, we’ll put the plants in water, like making tea. We’ll add lots of honey for sweetness and to feed the wild yeast. We’ll let the yeast eat for a few days. As the yeast eats the sugar, it will make our soda fizz and add some flavor. | This is actually how American root beer was originally made! 18th century colonists in North America made it by fermenting the root bark of the sassafras tree with some spices in a sugar and water mixture. Jamaica is also famous for making many kinds of root beers out of native plants. | One more example - tepache is a Mexican drink historically made out of corn but now usually made with fermented pineapple skins. |  | Now let’s try making our own soda. Let’s take a few minutes in the garden to harvest some ingredients and then we’ll make our sodas inside. (5 min) | Show students some options for harvesting. Harvest and then rinse off in the kitchen. | Follow task cards to complete the recipe. (15 min) |  | Closing ritual with SEL component: (5 min) | Try some pre-made sodas. Have students guess what the ingredients are. |  | Why is it important to try to make healthy choices? What are some other ways to practice self-love? | 

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS:

You could age this down for elementary by simplifying the microbiome explanation or skipping altogether; the recipe itself is easy for any age.

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED:
Students can make a wild bug for the next class or feed the instructor's bug; instructions are included in the task cards.
Students can work on recipe proposals.

Materials: 
Lesson visuals
Mason jars with lids
Cheesecloths or thin scrap fabric squares
Water, unchlorinated if possible. Hot water optional.
Mix of herbs, fruits and flowers - straight from the garden/organic is best
Honey
Task cards for students and instructor
Tepache take home recipe

Prep work and setup:
Make some wild sodas in advance for tasting. See task cards. 
The cold method will be fastest and easiest. Possible garden ingredients: any fresh fruits or soft herbs like lemon balm, basil, mint, strawberries, passionflower, chamomile.
The hot method will have the most interesting flavors. Possible garden ingredients: dried or woody herbs and roots like lavender, rosemary, ginger, dried hibiscus.
Make a ginger bug or other wild bug following task cards. Bugs need 3-4 days to ferment before use and hot sodas may need up to 1-2 weeks further fermentation to be finished brewing.
Brew some concentrated hot method options (ginger, hibiscus, etc) the morning of lesson and allow to cool to room temperature.
Mark areas where students can harvest from. Order extra ingredients if needed.
Edit task cards to reflect your ingredient options for students and print


Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Tepache
Microbiome
Basic soda-making guide
The Art of Fermentation by Sandor Katz Chapter 6
Be careful!! These beverages can have a lot of pressure and can explode glass jars if you do not monitor them closely. Sodas should be burped daily until they are strained and refrigerated. Ginger beer can keep fermenting in the fridge even after being strained, so enjoy within a few weeks.
Stir sodas every day to avoid mold growth on ingredients exposed to air (sticking out of the water).
Sodas that are left too long to ferment will become alcoholic or vinegary. Pay attention to fermentation time as written on task cards. Once they are strained and refrigerated, they will be good for at least 3 months (except ginger beer).
Make sure students get to drink their sodas before they go bad!

Ask: what do you think worms might eat for breakfast?  Help students out with prompts if needed: do worms eat cereal? Tin foil? Takis? Yogurt? Leaves? | Hold up a copy of the Worm Breakfast recipe so everyone can see.  Show them a few more things worms might eat: sticks, soil, leaves, rotten fruit, and water. | The worms are getting hungry!  Time for us to make them a big pot of worm breakfast. |  | Engaging activity (15 minutes): | Today, we are going to measure ingredients using cup measures.  This will help us practice the skills we will need for cooking class this year. | Hand out 3 measuring cups and a copy of the recipe to each table.  Ask students to work together to match the fractions on the cups to the fractions in the recipe. | Once most students seem confident enough to start measuring, model how to measure correctly.  Show an example of a cup measure that is underfilled, and ask the class “Is this full?”.  No, it is not full.  Show students an example of a cup that is overfilled.  “Is this full?”  If students respond yes, agree but show them how it is a little too full.  Show how to carefully scrape the top off back into the bowl so that it is perfectly flat at the top. | Model how to add the ingredient to the bowl, mix it in, and then pass it to the next person to measure and add another ingredient. | Hand out compost materials and ask one student to start at each table, and show the direction to pass the bowl once they finish measuring. |  | Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes): | Clean up–pack up materials and wipe tables. | Ask the class: what do worms eat for breakfast?  What goes in the compost? | Who here was kind today in class? Congratulate teams or students that were especially kind to each other in class. | Give out hand sanitizer and a fruit tasting. “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
K/1: put a large tray of compostable materials on each table, and explain that everyone is going to get a turn to pick one thing to add to the breakfast bowl, mix it in, and then pass it to the next person at their table.  Add water last!  For kindergarten, I found that no recipe is needed, but 1st grade can benefit from a simple ingredient list.  

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
If students finish early, bring out the worm bins and have students look for worms.  Show them how to gently feed them the worm breakfast.  Emphasizes kindness to others!!

Materials: 
4-5 old kitchen bowls or other large vessels for mixing
Spades for mixing
worm breakfast recipe, one per table
3 measuring cups (I suggest 1 cup, ½ cup, ¼ cup to start)
Sheet protector for recipes
Disposable bowls for recipe ingredients
Watering can/s
Plastic trays (optional, but useful for setup!)

Prep work and setup:
Setup trays with compost ingredients bowls.  Set aside watering cans with water.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Note: students may not have worked with fractions yet!  Walk around and help them identify which cup to use for each ingredient, and repeat often that “1-2” is also known as one half, etc, etc.

Worm Study 


[Table]
Summary: | Students will study worms in an indoor worm bin, draw what they see, and do the worm dance. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will learn what worms need to thrive in a worm bin and will understand that worms play an important role in the garden. | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness: | This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by allowing students to learn about worms through dance, hands on observation, drawing, and writing.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills: | This lesson promotes self-management as students take small risks in holding and interacting with worms. This lesson also promotes relationship skills as students display care and calm around garden animals.


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow: | Opening ritual: | Today we are going to be meeting one of the animals that lives in the garden. Give students clues about worms (i.e. this animal doesn’t have any legs, this animal is very wiggly). We are learning about worms! | Teach students with worm dance about what worms do in the garden. | Munch, munch! | (hand up, making eating gesture) | Wiggle, wiggle! | (wiggle your whole body) | Poop, poop! | (knees bending on the words) | Soil! | (arms up, big happy gesture) | Talk to the students about the rules of meeting the worms. We have to be gentle with them, because they are animals. We can hold the worms in our hands or on our popsicle sticks, but be sure to dig slowly and carefully with your popsicle stick. | Today we are going to practice kindness as we meet the worms. Ask students how they would feel if they were a worm - “would you like it if a big giant screamed at you, stepped on you, or took you away from your home?” | Engaging activity: | Divide class into small groups, assign each to a tray. Place a generous handful of worms, along with their food and bedding, in each tray. |  | Hand out paper, pencils, and popsicle sticks for stirring in the worm bin. | Encourage students to touch or hold the worms, making sure they are being gentle. | Can you draw what you see? What parts do you see on the worm? Which end is their head and which end is their tail? Do they have eyes? Why do you think there is garbage in their home? What do you think they’re eating? If you can, add labels to your drawing. | If you would like, hand out hand lenses. | Now you can look at one part of your worm very closely with this hand lens and draw what you see. | Walk around and help students label their drawings. |  | Have the students write a sentence about what they think worms like to eat based on their observations. | Closing ritual: | Wash hands. Have students come back to their seats. | Allow students to share about what they saw in the worm trays. Write their answers on the board. | Thank students for practicing kindness today and being gentle with the worms. Give shoutouts to students who were especially kind. Celebrate students who took a risk and went out of their comfort zone today. | Finish with a tasting. Remind students about the worm dance, worms eat out leftovers and make soil! If we don’t finish our tasting we can give the leftovers to the worms! Giving food to the worms is one way we can show our thanks and be kind to them!

Materials: 
Pencils
Paper
Clipboards
Worm bin
Trays
Hand lenses
Popsicle sticks
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Put blank papers on clipboards for students to draw on. Put scoops from the worm bin in trays. 

Anticipated problems:
Some students might be nervous around the worms. Tell students worms don’t have any teeth, they can’t hurt us. Try to encourage students to hold the worms. Make sure students hold worms over the trays, tell them not to walk around with the worms so they don’t drop them. 

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
If time allows, have students prepare scraps to put inside the worm bin. Students can use scissors to cut strips of newspaper in the worm bin, crush eggshells with a mortar and pestle, or cut up old leaves and weeds from the garden for the worms. 
For 3k-pk, you can also prepare “sensory trays” for students to extend the activity (as they probably will be less interested in observational drawings).  Before handing out worm bins, put trays of vegetables and flowers, seed packets, and other things students can touch with at their tables.  Show students how to use their popsicle sticks and magnifying glasses to explore the items, and once they’ve mastered the sensory trays, hand out worm bins.  
Task cards or handouts used: None.

Worms and Compost


[Table]
Summary: | Students will examine the worm bin, discuss the importance of healthy soil, and complete a worm-related worksheet. | 


[Table]
Objectives: |  | Students will demonstrate their knowledge of soil and compost.


[Table]
Core Competencies: | Garden skills and related academic content | 


[Table]
Cultural Responsiveness | : | This lesson communicates high expectations by having students explore their own live worm trays, positions the teacher as facilitator as students work in small groups to complete their worm observations.


[Table]
Social-Emotional Skills |  | Bravery, kindness, respect, self-management (safety), collaboration. This lesson promotes relationship skills as students practice displaying calm and care towards garden animals.


[Table]

[Table]
How Does This Lesson Promote Critical Thinking/Independence? | Students will have to use their investigative skills and lean on prior knowledge about worms, soil, compost and what they know about gardens to make assertions about what they are observing in the worm bins.


[Table]
Tags: | compost, worms, soil, garden | 


[Table]
Agenda/Class Flow |  |  | Opening Ritual (10 min) | Welcome students to the garden and introduce or review expectations and norms. | Today will be all about compost. Ask students what they already know about compost--where have they heard that word before?  What belongs in the compost bin?  What doesn’t belong?  Show or discuss examples. | Explain that things that can go into the compost break down.  Show students compost from bins 1, 2, and 3. | Do you notice anything living in the compost?  There are lots of garden animals who live in the compost, and they help break down the big stuff in our compost bin into smaller and smaller pieces, eventually making compost that we can use in the garden to help our plants grow big and tall. | One of those garden animals is worms!  Tell students that they will be observing the compost and the worms as they make compost for our plants. | Review the rules for observing the worms (students can use popsicle sticks to stir and look for worms, students may hold worms, be gentle and kind to the worms, don’t walk around with the worms and keep them over the tray). |  | Engaging Activity (20 min) | Hand out worksheets on clipboards.  Explain that we will be working in pairs today to observe worms and answer the questions on the worksheet. | Hand out trays and popsicle sticks to each table.  Have students observe trays filled with material from the worm bins. Circulate and help students with worksheets as needed. | If students finish worksheets early, hand out bins with materials to make a worm hotel.  What do worms need to live?  Have students create a habitat for their worms. | When finished, wash hands and return to their seats to complete a compost worksheet. |  | Closing Ritual (10 min) | Last week, we discussed actions we can take as gardeners that can help our community.  Why do you think composting is important for our neighborhood community? (Provide leading questions if needed, i.e. what happens to our trash if it doesn’t get composted?  Where does it go?  What would our garden look like without compost?). | Congratulate students on their good work today. | Finish with a seasonal tasting. |
